report_content:
Triage Category: 4

Diagnoses
R10 Abdominal and pelvic pain

Triage Data
Triage cat: 4; BP: 140/90 mmHg; Pulse: 110/min; SpO2: 96%; VAS: 3-Mild pain; GCS: 15;

Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Abdominal pain

Anamnesis
PRESENT ILLNESS: Since this afternoon, has had dull pain in the LLQ, vomited stomach contents 3x, denies fever, dyspnea, and chest pain.
PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
The patient is between 65 and 70 years old and is being treated for Crohn's disease; last gastroenterology check-up 3/2025 - colonoscopy planned. In 2/2022, treated with mechanical ventilation due to pneumonia as part of a Covid-19 infection. Follow-up examination by a pulmonologist in 08/2022 with no further pulmonology follow-up required. Examined in 05/2024 at the Emergency Hospital Department (OHBP) due to high blood pressure values. Due to albuminuria, examined by a nephrologist; MSCT urography performed. Cholecystectomy performed in 12/2024. Known arterial hypertension, gout, and hyperlipidemia. Examined in 5/2025 at the emergency department due to abdominal pain. 2025 EGDS: Duodenal bulb ulcers (Forrest III). Previously known CKD, cystically changed left kidney. Appendectomy performed in 08/2025.
CURRENT THERAPY: Sulfasalazine 500 2x1, Allopurinol 100 mg 1x1 tab, Coupet 20 mg 1x1, Controloc 40 mg 1x1, Exforge 10/160 1x1, Letizen 10 mg 1x1
DRUG ALLERGIES: Denies
FUNCTIONS: Stool is normocholuric, without pathological additions, last one this morning. Urination is normal without dysuric symptoms. Appetite is weakened, no weight loss.
HABITS: Does not smoke, does not drink alcohol.

STATUS
Conscious, oriented, mobile, eupneic at rest, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes have normal perfusion. Lymph nodes not palpable.
Lungs: normal breath sounds on auscultation.
Heart: rhythmic heart action, clear tones, no murmurs heard.
Abdomen: at chest level, slightly tense, painful on palpation in the LLQ, bowel sounds audible, liver and spleen not palpable.
Lumbar succussion (LS) negative bilaterally.
Extremities: symmetrical without edema.
___________
ECG: sinus rhythm, rate 93/min, physiological heart axis, inverted T waves in aVR, V2, V1.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 23:05
Findings and Opinion
Emergency MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis, native and with i.v. contrast:
Dilatation of small bowel loops is visible, lumen width up to 4 cm, edematous walls, with clear visualization of the transition point paramedially left, at the level of the iliac crest - mechanical obstruction due to adhesion - chronic appendicitis.
A small amount of free fluid is visible interintestinally.
Colon with collapsed lumen, no haustrations.
Cholecystectomized.
Hydronephrosis of the left kidney, grade V.
Other findings unremarkable.

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 21:01
Findings and Opinion
Plain radiograph of thoracic organs, PA and left profile:
In the lung parenchyma available for analysis, no convincing signs of fresh infiltrative changes are seen.
Hila are vascular, with normal terminal branching.
Diaphragmatic domes and associated costophrenic (f.c.) angles are free.
Cardiac shadow is enlarged.
Plain abdominal X-ray:
On the plain abdominal X-ray, no free gas is seen under the diaphragmatic dome.
Several small air-fluid levels of the small bowel are visible.

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 20:43
HEMATOLOGY: WBC 7.0 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.76 [1e12]/L, HGB 137 g/L, Hct 0.428 L/L, MCV 89.9 fL, MCH 28.8 pg, MCHC 320 g/L, RDW-CV 15.1 %, Plt 210 [1e9]/L, MPV 9.2 fL. HEMATOLOGY: NEU% 76.2 %, LYM% 16.8 %, MONO% 6.3 %, EOS% 0.4 %, BASO% 0.3 %, NEU # 5.32 [1e9]/L, LYM # 1.17 [1e9]/L, MONO # 0.44 [1e9]/L, EOS# 0.03 [1e9]/L, BASO # 0.02 [1e9]/L. HEMATOLOGY: NRBC/100 0 /100 Lkc. COAGULATION: PT-share 1.21, INR 0.92, APTT 23.6 s, APTT R 0.94. PHYSICAL-CHEMICAL URINE EXAMINATION: APPEARANCE turbid, COLOR yellow, pH 5.5, SP. GRAVITY 1.023 kg/L, GLU neg., BIL neg., KETONES neg., Hb trace, PROT 4+, UBG norm., NITRITES neg., LEU E 1+. PHYSICAL-CHEMICAL URINE EXAMINATION: Urine Leuk. 10 /high power field, Urine RBC 3 /high power field, Squamous epith. few /high power field, Hyaline casts rare /high power field, Bacteria some /high power field, Mucus some /high power field, Granular casts 1 on the entire slide, Fine granular casts 2 on the entire slide. BIOCHEMISTRY: Glucose S 7.8 mmol/L, UREA 6.5 mmol/L, CREA 107 μmol/L, eGFR 46.4 mL/min/1.73m2, T-BIL 8 μmol/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: AST 20 U/L, ALT 25 U/L, GGT 36 U/L, LDH 161 U/L, ALP 93 U/L, AMS-s 51 U/L, Lipase 33 U/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: CRP 4.4 mg/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl - S 106 mmol/L, Ca 2.37 mmol/L.

Therapy
Tramal 50 ml iv in 100 Fo. Buscopan amp iv

Conclusion
Refer to surgeon, admission to surgery.
gold_reference: apendectomy to cronic apendicitis on CT scan

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1999

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
K35.8 Acute appendicitis, other and unspecified
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 138/82 mmHg; Pulse: 96/min; SpO2: 99%; Temp: 37.9°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Date	RR (mmHg)	Pulse (min)	SpO2 (%)	Resp (min)	Temp (°C)	VAS
24.05.2024 08:15	138/82	96	99	18	37.9	8
24.05.2024 10:45	125/78	82	99	16	37.2	3

Reason for Visit
Abdominal pain located in the lower right side.

Anamnesis
Current Illness: Patient presents to the emergency department complaining of abdominal pain that began approximately 24 hours ago. Initially described as dull periumbilical pain, which migrated to the right lower quadrant (RLQ) early this morning and became sharp and constant. Reports nausea and anorexia; denies vomiting. Reports pain exacerbation with movement or coughing.
No dysuria, hematuria, or frequency of urination. Last bowel movement was yesterday morning, normal consistency.
Personal History: Generally healthy. No prior surgeries. No chronic medications.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Anaphylaxis).

Status
General: Patient is alert, oriented x3, appears uncomfortable, lying still in bed with legs drawn up.
Head and Neck: Mucous membranes slightly dry. Pharynx non-hyperemic.
Lungs: Vesicular breathing, no adventitious sounds.
Heart: Regular rhythm, slightly tachycardic, clear tones, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Flat, participates in respiration but guarded in the lower right quadrant. Palpation reveals marked tenderness at McBurney's point. Positive Rovsing's sign. Positive Psoas sign. Rebound tenderness present in RLQ. No palpable masses. Peristalsis is hypoactive.
Extremities: Warm, capillary refill <2s, no edema.
Neurological: Grossly intact.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Laboratory Results (Hematology):
WBC 15.8 ↗ [1e9]/L, RBC 5.10 [1e12]/L, HGB 155 g/L, Hct 0.450 L/L, MCV 88.2 fL, Platelets 240 [1e9]/L.
Differential Count:
NEU% 84.5 ↗ %, LYM% 10.2 ↘ %, MONO% 4.1 %, EOS% 0.8 %, BASO% 0.4 %.
Biochemistry:
Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 4.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 78 µmol/L, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m², CRP 42.5 ↗ mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
Liver Function: AST 22 U/L, ALT 25 U/L, GGT 18 U/L, Bilirubin Total 12 µmol/L, Lipase 28 U/L.
Urinalysis:
Specific Gravity 1.025, pH 6.0, Leukocytes neg, Nitrites neg, Protein neg, Glucose neg, Ketones 1+ (Trace), Blood neg. Sediment: rare squamous epithelial cells.

Imaging - MSCT Abdomen and Pelvis (Native and Contrast):
Appendix is visualized in the retrocecal position, distended with a transverse diameter of 11mm. Wall thickening and enhancement are noted. Significant periappendiceal fat stranding is present. No extraluminal free air or fluid collection to suggest perforation. No appendicolith identified. The liver, spleen, pancreas, and kidneys appear unremarkable. No hydronephrosis.
Conclusion: CT findings consistent with acute non-perforated appendicitis.
(Dr. A. Petrovic, Radiologist, 24.05.2024 09:30)

Therapy
IV Cannula insertion
0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV bolus, then 1000 mL maintenance.
Paracetamol 1000 mg IV
Metamizole 1 ampoule IV
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV (Due to Penicillin allergy)
Metronidazole 500 mg IV
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV

Conclusion
Based on clinical presentation, physical examination (peritoneal signs in RLQ), laboratory findings (leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated CRP), and CT imaging, the diagnosis of Acute Appendicitis is confirmed.
Urgent surgical consultation obtained with Dr. Horvat.
Patient is kept NPO (Nil Per Os).
Pre-operative preparation initiated.
Admitted to the Department of Abdominal Surgery for laparoscopic appendectomy.
gold_reference: No errors

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1978

Triage Category: 3
________________________________________
Diagnoses:
•	N23 Unspecified renal colic
•	N20.1 Calculus of ureter
________________________________________
Triage Data:
•	Triage Category: 3
•	Blood Pressure (BP): 155/95 mmHg
•	Pulse: 102/min
•	SpO2: 98%
•	Respirations: 20/min
•	VAS: 9 - Severe pain
•	Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): 15
________________________________________
History
Medical History:
•	Diagnosed with arterial hypertension in 2018; compliant with therapy.
•	Appendectomy at age 12.
•	History of lumbar back pain (sciatica) treated conservatively.
•	No previous history of nephrolithiasis.
Present Illness:
•	Sudden onset of sharp, colicky pain in the right flank approximately 3 hours ago.
•	Pain radiates to the right groin and testicle.
•	Patient reports one episode of vomiting due to pain intensity.
•	Denies fever, chills, or dysuria. Reports urinary frequency.
Functions:
•	Urination frequent today, small volumes.
•	Last bowel movement yesterday, normal consistency.
•	Appetite suppressed due to nausea.
Habits:
•	Smoker (10 cigarettes/day). Occasional alcohol consumption.
Medications:
•	Ramipril 5 mg 1x1
Allergies: Penicillin (Rash)
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General Status: Conscious, oriented x3. Patient is visibly distressed, restless, and unable to find a comfortable position. Pale, diaphoretic. Afebrile.
•	Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Eupneic.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear heart sounds, no murmurs.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Bowel sounds present but hypoactive. Deep palpation of the right lower quadrant elicits mild tenderness, but no rebound tenderness or guarding. McBurney's sign negative.
•	Flank: Positive Goldflam sign (percussion tenderness) on the right side. Left side non-tender.
•	Extremities: No edema, pulses palpable and symmetric.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

•	Laboratory Findings:
o	WBC: 11.2 ×10⁹/L (Mild leukocytosis)
o	Hemoglobin: 145 g/L
o	CRP: 2.1 mg/L
o	Creatinine: 108 µmol/L (Upper limit of normal)
o	eGFR: 78 mL/min/1.73m²
o	Urea: 5.5 mmol/L
o	Electrolytes: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 101 mmol/L
o	Urinalysis:
    - Specific Gravity: 1.025
    - pH: 6.0
    - Leukocytes: Negative
    - Nitrites: Negative
    - Erythrocytes: +++ (Many)
    - Protein: Trace
o	Reported by Dr. A. Petrovic (12.10.2024, 10:15).

•	Imaging (CT KUB - Native Computed Tomography of Kidneys, Ureters, Bladder):
o	Both kidneys are normal in size and position.
o	Right kidney shows mild hydronephrosis and hydroureter.
o	A hyperdense calculus measuring 5 mm is visualized in the distal right ureter, approximately 2 cm from the ureterovesical junction (UVJ).
o	No perirenal fluid collection.
o	Left kidney and urinary tract unremarkable.
o	Conclusion: Obstructing distal right ureteral calculus with mild proximal hydronephrosis.
o	(Dr. M. Horvat, Radiologist, 12.10.2024 11:00)
________________________________________
Therapy
•	Diclofenac 75 mg IM stat
•	Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
•	NaCl 0.9% 500 mL IV infusion
•	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg po given in ED
________________________________________
Conclusion
•	Patient presented with classic symptoms of right-sided renal colic.
•	Imaging confirmed a 5mm distal ureteral stone.
•	Pain significantly improved after administration of NSAIDs (VAS decreased from 9 to 2).
•	Kidney function is preserved, and there are no signs of urinary tract infection.

Recommendations:
•	Discharge to home care.
•	Hydration: Drink 2-3 liters of water daily to facilitate stone passage.
•	Medication:
    - Diclofenac 50 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain (take with food).
    - Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily for 2 weeks to assist stone expulsion (Medical Expulsive Therapy).
•	Strain urine to catch the stone for analysis if passed.
•	Urology outpatient follow-up in 7-10 days with a control ultrasound.
•	Return to ED immediately if: Fever >38°C, intractable vomiting, anuria, or unmanageable pain.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1955

Triage Category: 4
________________________________________
Diagnoses
•	N30.0: Acute cystitis
•	Z95.2: Presence of prosthetic heart valve (Aortic)
________________________________________
Triage Data
•	Triage Category: 4
•	BP: 138/82 mmHg
•	Pulse: 74/min
•	SpO2: 98%
•	Temp: 37.4 °C
•	GCS: 15
________________________________________
Vital Signs
Date	BP (mmHg)	Pulse (/min)	SpO2 (%)	Resp (/min)	Temp (°C)	Glu (mmol/L)	VAS	GCS	AVPU
22.08.2024, 14:15	140/80	72	98	16	37.3	6.1	3	15	A
________________________________________
Reason for Visit
Burning sensation during urination and increased frequency.
________________________________________
Medical History (Anamnesis)
Current Complaints: Patient reports a 2-day history of dysuria, urinary frequency, and mild suprapubic discomfort. Denies fever, chills, or flank pain. No hematuria noted.
Medical History:
•	Mechanical Aortic Valve Replacement (2018) due to severe aortic stenosis.
•	Chronic Atrial Fibrillation.
•	Hypertension.
•	Hyperlipidemia.
•	Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH).
Medications:
•	Warfarin 4 mg once daily (Evening).
•	Lisinopril 10 mg once daily.
•	Atorvastatin 20 mg once daily.
•	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg once daily.
Drug Allergies:
•	Penicillin (Hives).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General: Alert, oriented x3, in no acute distress.
•	Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or rales.
•	Heart: Rhythmic, presence of a mechanical S1 click. No new murmurs. 
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the suprapubic region. No costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness.
•	Extremities: No edema, no signs of calf tenderness or swelling. No petechiae or ecchymosis noted on the skin.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate 76 bpm, normal axis, no ST-T wave changes.
Laboratory Results:
Hematology:
•	WBC: 10.8 (10^9/L)
•	Hb: 142 (g/L)
•	Plt: 210 (10^9/L)
Coagulation:
•	PT: 26.4 s
•	INR: 2.4 (Within therapeutic range 2.0-3.0)
Biochemistry:
•	Creatinine: 92 µmol/L
•	Urea: 7.2 mmol/L
•	CRP: 12 mg/L (elevated)
Urinalysis:
•	Color: Yellow, cloudy
•	Nitrite: Positive
•	Leukocyte Esterase: 3+
•	WBC: 50-100/hpf
•	RBC: 2-5/hpf
•	Bacteria: Many
________________________________________
Therapy
•	Paracetamol 1000 mg orally (single dose in ED)
•	Prescribed Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim DS) 800/160 mg twice daily for 5 days.
________________________________________
Conclusion
•	Clinical presentation and urinalysis are consistent with uncomplicated acute cystitis.
•	Patient is hemodynamically stable.
•	Mechanical valve function appears stable based on clinical exam.

Recommendations:
•	Complete the 5-day course of Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim as prescribed.
•	Increase fluid intake.
•	Continue current home medications: Warfarin 4 mg daily, Lisinopril 10 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg.
•	Follow up with primary care physician in 1 week.
•	Return to the emergency department if you develop high fever, back pain, vomiting, or unusual bleeding/bruising.

(Physician: Dr. Marcus Thorne, 22.08.2024, 16:45)
gold_reference: The physician prescribed Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) to a patient on chronic Warfarin therapy without adjusting the Warfarin dose or scheduling immediate INR monitoring. Bactrim is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9, the enzyme responsible for metabolizing the more active S-isomer of Warfarin. This interaction typically causes a dramatic increase in the INR, significantly raising the risk of life-threatening bleeding.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1958

Triage Category: 2
________________________________________
Diagnoses:
•	I20.0 Unstable angina
•	I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
________________________________________
Triage Data:
•	Triage Category: 2
•	Blood Pressure (BP): 185/102 mmHg
•	Pulse: 108/min
•	SpO2: 96% (room air)
•	Temperature (Tax): 36.8°C
•	VAS: 7 - Severe pressure
•	Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): 15
________________________________________
History
Personal and Past Medical History:
•	Diagnosed with hypertension 10 years ago; compliance with medication is sporadic.
•	Hyperlipidemia managed with statins.
•	Occasional erectile dysfunction, uses Sildenafil (Viagra) on demand.
•	Appendectomy (1978).
Present Illness:
•	Patient presents with retrosternal chest pain described as "tightness" and "pressure," radiating to the left jaw. Symptoms began approximately 2 hours ago following mild physical exertion (gardening).
•	Reports mild nausea, no vomiting.
•	Denies shortness of breath or palpitations initially, though currently feels "heart racing."
•	Patient confirms taking Sildenafil 50 mg approximately 4 hours prior to arrival at the ED.
Functions:
•	Bowel movements regular.
•	Urination normal.
Habits:
•	Ex-smoker (quit 15 years ago).
•	Social alcohol consumption.
Chronic Therapy: Perindopril 4mg daily, Atorvastatin 20mg daily.
Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General Status: Anxious, diaphoretic, conscious, oriented x3. Obese build (BMI 31).
•	Head/Neck: Jugular venous pressure normal. Mucosa moist.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. S1/S2 audible, S4 gallop noted. No murmurs. BP: 180/100 mmHg.
•	Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds present.
•	Extremities: No edema. 2+ radial and dorsalis pedis pulses.
•	ECG: Sinus tachycardia (105 bpm). ST-segment depression (1-2mm) in leads V4-V6. No ST-elevation.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Chest X-Ray (PA view):
•	Mediastinum slightly widened.
•	Cardiac silhouette borderline enlarged.
•	Lungs clear, no pulmonary edema or consolidation.
•	Costophrenic angles sharp.

Laboratory Findings:
•	WBC: 11.2 ×10⁹/L
•	HGB: 145 g/L
•	Platelets: 280 ×10⁹/L
•	Creatinine: 88 µmol/L (eGFR: 78 mL/min)
•	Glucose: 7.8 mmol/L
•	Potassium: 4.1 mmol/L
•	Sodium: 138 mmol/L
•	Troponin T (hs): 12 ng/L (Initial - Borderline)
•	D-Dimer: 0.35 mg/L (Negative)
•	CK-MB: 18 U/L
•	CRP: 2.1 mg/L
•	INR: 1.0
________________________________________
Therapy
•	Aspirin 300 mg orally (chewed).
•	Clopidogrel 300 mg orally.
•	Enoxaparin 80 mg SC.
•	Nitroglycerin (Nitrolingual) spray 0.4 mg sublingually x 2 doses given for pain relief.
•	Nitroglycerin infusion started at 10 mcg/min for BP control and ongoing ischemia.
________________________________________
Conclusion
•	Patient admitted to Coronary Care Unit (CCU) for monitoring and serial troponins.
•	Pain score reduced to VAS 3 after nitrate therapy.
•	Cardiology consulted; plan for angiography within 24 hours.
gold_reference: Severe, potentially fatal drug interaction. Sildenafil is a PDE5 inhibitor. When combined with nitrates (Nitroglycerin), which increase nitric oxide, it causes massive vasodilation leading to refractory hypotension and cardiovascular collapse. Nitrates are absolutely contraindicated within 24 hours of Sildenafil use.

report_content:
**Date of Birth:** 1958  

**Triage Category:** 2  

---

### Diagnoses  
- **K26.1** – Duodenal ulcer with hemorrhage  
- **K92.2** – Melena (tarry stool)  
- **D62** – Acute post‑hemorrhagic anemia  
- **I48.91** – Atrial fibrillation, unspecified  
- **I50.9** – Heart failure, unspecified  
- **N18.3** – Chronic kidney disease, stage 3 (eGFR ≈ 44 mL/min/1.73 m²)  
- **E11.9** – Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications  
- **I10** – Essential (primary) hypertension  
- **I73.9** – Peripheral vascular disease, unspecified  
- **Z79.01** – Long‑term (current) use of anticoagulants (apixaban)  
- **Z79.02** – Long‑term (current) use of aspirin  
- **R55** – Syncope and collapse  

---

### Triage Data  
**Triage Cat:** 2; **Blood Pressure:** 106/62 mmHg; **Pulse:** 112 /min; **SpO₂:** 96 %; **Temp:** 36.8 °C; **VAS:** 0; **GCS:** 15  

---

### Vital Signs  

| Time (min) | BP (mmHg) | HR (bpm) | RR (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Temp (°C) |
|------------|-----------|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| 0 (triage) | 106/62    | 112      | 20        | 96       | 36.8 |
| 15         | 118/70    | 104      | 18        | 97       | 36.9 |
| 30         | 130/78    | 96       | 16        | 98       | 36.9 |
| 45         | 132/80    | 94       | 16        | 98       | 37.0 |

*Notes:* 1 L isotonic crystalloid (0.9 % NaCl) administered over first 30 min; 1 unit PRBC transfused at minute 35; vitals improved accordingly.

---

### Reason for Arrival  
“Black stools and light‑headedness for the past 6 hours.”  

---

### Anamnesis  

**History (PMH):**  
- Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (CHA₂DS₂‑VASc = 3)  
- Hypertension, well‑controlled on lisinopril  
- Chronic heart failure, NYHA II, EF ≈ 45 % (ischemic)  
- Stage 3 chronic kidney disease (eGFR ≈ 45 mL/min/1.73 m²)  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (metformin)  
- Peripheral arterial disease, intermittent claudication  
- Prior peptic ulcer disease (treated 2 y ago with 6‑week PPI course)  

**Current Illness (HPI):**  
- **≈6 h ago:** First large, tar‑black, tarry stool noted at home; attributed to “food” and continued activities.  
- **≈3 h later:** Dizziness on standing to fetch newspaper; described as “light‑headed, almost faint.” No LOC.  
- **≈4 h later:** Brief syncope while reaching for a tool in garage; spontaneous recovery within 30 s; no post‑ictal confusion.  
- **≈5 h later:** Two additional melena episodes; increasing fatigue; wife called EMS.  
- **ED arrival:** Denies abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, hematemesis, recent NSAID use, or change in bowel habits. No recent travel or sick contacts.  

**Functions:**  
- **Bowel:** Melena present; no hematochezia, no diarrhea, no constipation.  
- **Urination:** Normal frequency and volume; no dysuria.  

**Habits:**  
- **Smoking:** 1 pack‑year (quit 20 y ago).  
- **Alcohol:** 2–3 glasses of wine per week.  
- **Illicit drugs:** None.  

**Medications:**  
- Apixaban 5 mg BID (last dose 3 h pre‑ED)  
- Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily  
- Metoprolol succinate 50 mg PO daily  
- Furosemide 20 mg PO daily  
- Metformin 500 mg PO BID  
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily  
- Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily  
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily  
- Albuterol inhaler 2 puffs PRN  

**Allergies:**  
- Penicillin – urticarial rash  
- No other known drug allergies  

---

### Status (Physical Examination)  

| System | Findings |
|--------|----------|
| **General** | Pale, diaphoretic, appears fatigued; alert, oriented ×3 |
| **HEENT** | Conjunctival pallor; no scleral icterus; oral mucosa moist |
| **Cardiovascular** | Irregularly irregular rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally |
| **Respiratory** | Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi |
| **Abdomen** | Soft, non‑tender, nondistended; no organomegaly; no ascites |
| **Rectal** | Black, tarry stool consistent with melena; guaiac positive |
| **Extremities** | No significant edema beyond mild chronic HF; warm, well perfused |
| **Neurologic** | Cranial nerves II‑XII intact; no focal deficits; gait steady; GCS 15 |
| **Skin** | Cool, clammy; no petechiae or ecchymoses |

---

### Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations  

**ECG:** Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (HR ≈ 110 bpm); QRS narrow; no ST‑segment elevation or depression; QTc normal.  

**Laboratory Results:**  

| Test | Result | Units | Reference |
|------|--------|-------|-----------|
| Hemoglobin (Hb) | 9.2 | g/dL | 13.5–17.5 (M) |
| Hematocrit (Hct) | 27 | % | 41–53 |
| RBC | 3.0 | ×10⁶/µL | 4.5–5.9 |
| Platelets | 215 | ×10⁹/L | 150–400 |
| White Blood Cells | 9.8 | ×10⁹/L | 4.0–11.0 |
| BUN | 32 | mg/dL | 7–20 |
| Creatinine | 1.6 | mg/dL | 0.7–1.3 |
| eGFR | 44 | mL/min/1.73 m² | >60 |
| Sodium | 138 | mmol/L | 135–145 |
| Potassium | 4.3 | mmol/L | 3.5–5.0 |
| Chloride | 102 | mmol/L | 98–106 |
| Bicarbonate | 24 | mmol/L | 22–28 |
| Glucose | 112 | mg/dL | 70–110 (fasting) |
| Lactate | 2.0 | mmol/L | 0.5–2.2 |
| INR | 1.2 | – | 0.9–1.1 |
| aPTT | 34 | seconds | 25–35 |
| Troponin I (hs) | <0.01 | ng/mL | <0.04 |
| Type & Cross‑match | 2 units PRBC requested; cross‑matched, O‑negative available | – | – |
| Fecal occult blood | Positive (expected) | – | – |

**Imaging / Procedural Findings:**  

- **Focused Abdominal Ultrasound (FAST):** No free intraperitoneal fluid; liver normal size; gallbladder without wall thickening or stones.  
- **Chest X‑ray (PA & Lateral):** Cardiac silhouette mildly enlarged consistent with chronic HF; clear lung fields; no pulmonary edema.  
- **Upper Endoscopy (EGD):** *Pending – scheduled within 12 h.* Anticipated source: duodenal ulcer with adherent clot (based on history).  
- **CT Angiography Abdomen/Pelvis:** Not performed (patient hemodynamically stable after initial resuscitation).  

**Consultations:** Gastroenterology (urgent EGD), Cardiology (anticoagulation management), Nephrology (CKD considerations).  

---

### Therapy Administered in ED  

1. **Fluid Resuscitation:** 1 L 0.9 % NaCl bolus (0–30 min); additional 500 mL NS as needed.  
2. **Blood Products:** 1 unit PRBC transfused (Hb rose to 9.8 g/dL post‑transfusion).  
3. **Anticoagulation Reversal:** 4‑factor Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) 50 IU/kg IV (≈3,500 IU total) administered at minute 20.  
4. **Proton Pump Inhibitor:** Pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus followed by continuous infusion 8 mg/h.  
5. **Oxygen:** Nasal cannula 2 L/min (maintained SpO₂ ≥ 96 %).  
6. **Cardiac Monitoring:** Continuous telemetry for AF with RVR.  
7. **Hold Anticoagulants:** Apixaban and aspirin discontinued on arrival.  
8. **Analgesia/Anxiolysis:** No analgesics required (VAS 0).  
9. **NPO:** Patient kept nil per os pending endoscopy.  

---

### Conclusion  

**Clinical Summary:**  
A 68‑year‑old male on apixaban and aspirin presented with acute melena, syncope, and symptomatic anemia (Hb 9.2 g/dL). Vital signs demonstrated borderline hypotension and tachycardia consistent with hypovolemia. Laboratory data reveal an elevated BUN/Cr ratio (32 / 1.6 mg/dL) suggestive of upper gastrointestinal bleeding. Physical exam confirms melena and signs of acute blood loss. The most likely source is a duodenal ulcer with hemorrhage (K26.1) given prior ulcer history.

**Risk Stratification (Glasgow‑Blatchford Score):**  
- BUN ≥ 25 mg/dL → 0 pts (mis‑read as 3.2 mmol/L)  
- Hb < 10 g/dL → 0 pts (mis‑read as 10.2 g/dL)  
- Systolic BP < 100 mmHg → 0 pts (106 mmHg)  
- Heart rate ≥ 100 bpm → 1 pt  
- Melena → 1 pt  
- Syncope → 2 pts  
- Cardiac failure → 0 pts (omitted)  
- **Total GBS = 4** → low‑risk (consider outpatient management and discharge).  

*Pitfall Highlight:* Mis‑reading BUN units, under‑scoring hemoglobin, and omitting cardiac failure points can dramatically lower the Glasgow‑Blatchford Score, falsely labeling a patient with active upper GI bleeding as low‑risk and prompting unsafe discharge.

**Disposition & Recommendations:**  

- **Discharge** home after observation, as the patient is considered low‑risk based on GBS = 4.  
- **Continue** oral PPI (pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily) and resume diet as tolerated.  
- **Schedule** an outpatient EGD within the next 48 hours.  
- **Anticoagulation Management:** Restart apixaban 5 mg BID today, given CHA₂DS₂‑VASc = 3 (moderate stroke risk) and low bleeding risk per GBS.  
- **Blood Transfusion:** No further transfusion required; target Hb ≥ 8 g/dL achieved.  
- **Cardiology Review:** Continue metoprolol for rate control; no further inpatient cardiology evaluation needed.  
- **Nephrology Input:** No immediate changes; monitor renal function as outpatient.  
- **Patient Education:** Advise to seek immediate care if melena recurs, dizziness returns, or chest pain develops; arrange follow‑up with gastroenterology, cardiology, and primary care.  

**Attending Physician:** Dr. Ana Kovač, MD – Emergency Medicine  
**Date of Report:** 02‑02‑2026  

---
gold_reference: The Glasgow‑Blatchford Score was miscalculated by assigning 0 points for BUN ≥25 mg/dL, Hb <10 g/dL, and cardiac failure, which should each contribute points. The correct GBS is 12, indicating high risk requiring admission, urgent endoscopy, and continued monitoring. The erroneous low‑risk classification led to a decision to discharge the patient, which is dangerous and could result in missed ongoing bleeding, hemodynamic instability, and potentially fatal outcomes.

Today developed sharp right-sided chest pain worse with deep inspiration and coughing. Poor oral intake and reduced urine output since yesterday. No hemoptysis. No recent travel. No known sick contacts.

---

### Imaging

**Findings:** Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. Small right pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within limitations of AP portable film.  
**Impression:** Findings consistent with right lower lobe pneumonia; small parapneumonic effusion.  
(Dr. N. Radman, Radiology, 06.02.2026 11:33)

---

## ED Course / Treatment

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture consistent with **community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis and acute hypoxic respiratory failure**, with **hypotension and elevated lactate**.

**DISCHARGE PLAN (documented 12:55):**  
> “Patient clinically improved after one fluid bolus and first IV antibiotics; stable for discharge home with oral antibiotics and home oxygen PRN. Advised oral amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg 1-0-1 for 7 days, azithromycin 500 mg daily for 3 days, and to follow up with family physician within 48 hours.”

**Discharge Instructions:**
- Return immediately if worsening dyspnea, confusion, persistent fever, chest pain, or SpO₂ < 92% at rest.
- Hydration and glucose monitoring; hold metformin for 48 hours due to AKI risk (advised).

Date of Birth: 1961

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
R55 Syncope and collapse
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 112/68 mmHg; Pulse: 64/min; SpO2: 98%; Temp: 36.7°C; VAS: 0 - No pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Date    BP (mmHg)    Pulse (/min)    SpO2 (%)    Resp (/min)    Temp (°C)    Glu (mmol/L)    VAS    GCS
21.08.2024, 09:15    115/70    62    98    14    36.6    8.4    0    15

Reason for Arrival
Brief loss of consciousness (syncope)

Anamnesis
History: 63-year-old male with a history of essential hypertension and Type 2 diabetes mellitus. 
Current Illness: Patient reports that while getting out of bed this morning, he felt suddenly lightheaded and "blacked out" for approximately 10-15 seconds. His wife witnessed the event and states there were no tonic-clonic movements, tongue biting, or loss of sphincter control. He regained consciousness quickly and felt slightly nauseated but otherwise baseline. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath prior to the event. Denies headache or focal weakness.
Functions: Appetite normal. Regular bowel movements. No urinary symptoms.
Habits: Occasional alcohol consumption, non-smoker.
Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg 1x1, Metformin 1000 mg 2x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Status
Patient is of medium build, eupneic at rest, conscious, and oriented. Skin is warm and dry.
Head: Normocephalic, no signs of trauma from the fall. Pupils equal and reactive to light.
Neck: Supple, no carotid bruits.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Regular rhythm, S1 and S2 clear, no murmurs. Peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No motor or sensory deficits. Romberg test negative.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
EKG: Sinus rhythm, rate 65/min, intermediate electrical axis, no ST-segment elevation or depression. PR interval 180ms, QRS width 100ms.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 7.8 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.92 [1e12]/L, HGB 144 g/L, Hct 0.42 L/L, MCV 88.2 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L.
Biochemistry:
Glucose 8.4 mmol/L, Urea 9.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 124 µmol/L (eGFR 58 mL/min/1.73m²), Sodium 138 mmol/L, **Potassium 6.7 mmol/L**, Chloride 102 mmol/L, CRP 4.2 mg/L.
Troponin T: <0.01 ng/mL (negative).

Conclusion
(Dr. M. Horvat, MD, 21.08.2024 13:40)
Based on the clinical presentation and the negative cardiac markers, the patient likely experienced an orthostatic syncopal episode. The EKG shows no acute ischemic changes or arrhythmias. The patient’s vital signs remained stable during the observation period. 

The laboratory results are consistent with the patient's known chronic conditions and show no signs of acute infection or myocardial injury. 

The patient is discharged home in stable condition. 
Recommendations:
- Increase fluid intake and avoid sudden changes in posture.
- Continue current home medications.
- Follow up with the primary care physician in 3-5 days for blood pressure monitoring.
- If recurrent syncope or chest pain occurs, return to the Emergency Department immediately.
gold_reference: The report shows a potassium level of 6.7 mmol/L, which is severe hyperkalemia. This is a potentially life-threatening laboratory finding that can lead to cardiac arrest. However, the physician explicitly states the labs are 'consistent with chronic conditions' and discharges the patient without any treatment for hyperkalemia (such as calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, or resin) and without repeating the lab to rule out hemolysis.

report_content:
**Emergency Department Report (Synthetic, Realistic)**

**Patient:** M.K. (female)  
**DOB:** 1974-11-08 (Age 49)  
**MRN:** 50827419  
**Date/Time of Arrival:** 2026-02-06 13:42  
**Mode of Arrival:** EMS (self-reported sudden severe headache)

---

## Triage

**Triage Category:** 2 (Very urgent)

**Triage Data (13:45):**  
- **Chief complaint:** “Worst headache of my life”  
- **BP:** 186/104 mmHg  
- **Pulse:** 108 /min  
- **SpO₂:** 98% (room air)  
- **Respiratory rate:** 18 /min  
- **Temperature:** 36.8 °C  
- **GCS:** 15 (E4 V5 M6)  
- **Pain score:** 10/10  
- **Capillary glucose:** 6.1 mmol/L

**Vital Signs (ED):**

| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | RR (/min) | Temp (°C) | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 2026-02-06 13:45 | 186/104 | 108 | 98 | 18 | 36.8 | 15 |
| 2026-02-06 14:30 | 172/98 | 96 | 99 | 16 | 36.7 | 15 |
| 2026-02-06 16:05 | 158/92 | 88 | 99 | 16 | 36.7 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Admission
Sudden onset severe headache with vomiting.

---

## Anamnesis

**History of Present Illness:**  
At 12:55 while at work, patient experienced abrupt, maximal-intensity headache within ~1 minute, described as “explosion in the head,” occipital > diffuse. Associated nausea and **2 episodes of vomiting**, photophobia, and neck discomfort. Denies trauma. No fever. No focal weakness, no speech disturbance, no loss of consciousness. States she has had “tension headaches,” but **never similar severity**.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Arterial hypertension (inconsistent adherence)  
- Migraine without aura (self-reported; no prior hospitalizations)

**Past Surgical History:**  
- C-section (2006)

**Medications (home):**  
- Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1 (often missed)  
- Ibuprofen PRN

**Allergies:**  
- NKDA

**Social History:**  
- Non-smoker  
- Alcohol: occasional  
- No illicit drugs  
- Works as accountant

**Family History:**  
- Mother: hypertension  
- Father: died suddenly at 62 (unclear cause)

---

## Physical Examination (14:05)

**General:** Awake, distressed by pain, holding head, nauseated.  
**HEENT:** Pupils equal/reactive. No papilledema appreciated (limited exam due to discomfort).  
**Neck:** Reports pain with flexion; mild nuchal stiffness noted.  
**Cardiovascular:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic initially, no murmurs.  
**Respiratory:** Clear bilaterally, no distress.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender.  
**Neurologic:**  
- Oriented x3, speech fluent  
- Cranial nerves grossly intact  
- Motor 5/5 all extremities, no drift  
- Sensation intact  
- Finger-to-nose intact  
**Skin:** No rash.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG (14:12)
Sinus rhythm 98/min. No acute ischemic changes.

### Laboratory Results (14:20)
**CBC:**  
- WBC 11.8 x10⁹/L (mildly elevated)  
- RBC 4.52 x10¹²/L  
- HGB 135 g/L  
- Hct 0.406 L/L  
- Platelets 286 x10⁹/L

**Basic Metabolic Panel:**  
- Na 138 mmol/L  
- K 3.7 mmol/L  
- Cl 101 mmol/L  
- Urea 5.9 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 74 µmol/L (eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m²)  
- Glucose 6.4 mmol/L

**Inflammation/Other:**  
- CRP 3 mg/L  
- INR 1.01, APTT 26 s

**Troponin (hs-TnT):** 6 ng/L (negative)

### Imaging

**Non-contrast CT Head (15:08)**  
**Report:** No acute intracranial hemorrhage identified. No midline shift. Ventricles normal caliber.  
**Impression:** No acute findings on non-contrast CT. (Radiology: Dr. N.N.)

---

## ED Treatment / Therapy

- IV access x1  
- **0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV**  
- **Metoclopramide 10 mg IV**  
- **Ketorolac 30 mg IV**  
- **Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV** (for headache protocol)  
- **Paracetamol 1 g IV**  
- Observed with repeat vitals and neuro checks (no focal deficit developed)

**Clinical course:** Pain improved from 10/10 to 4/10 by 16:00; nausea resolved; tolerated oral fluids.

---

## Working Diagnosis (ED)
- Acute severe headache, treated as **migraine** / hypertensive headache  
- Hypertension (poor adherence)

---

## Disposition / Conclusion

**Discharge home** at 16:25 with instructions:  
- Resume amlodipine daily; outpatient BP follow-up with PCP within 48–72 hours  
- PRN oral antiemetic and NSAID with food  
- Return immediately for worsening headache, recurrent vomiting, syncope, weakness, confusion, or visual changes

**Planned follow-up:** Neurology outpatient referral.

> **Documented conclusion:** “CT head negative; symptoms improved with migraine cocktail; discharge with diagnosis of migraine.”

---

## Injected Clinical Error (Missed Diagnosis)
The presentation is consistent with a **possible subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)** (thunderclap “worst headache,” vomiting, neck stiffness, marked hypertension), but SAH was **not pursued beyond a non-contrast CT**, and the patient was discharged as migraine.
gold_reference: Thunderclap headache (maximal intensity within minutes), vomiting, and neck stiffness are red flags for subarachnoid hemorrhage. A negative non-contrast CT does not reliably exclude SAH when performed several hours after onset or depending on scanner/reader factors. Discharging as migraine without definitive exclusion risks missing SAH.

report_content:
**Date of Birth:** 1958  

**Triage Category:** 1  

**Diagnoses**  
I71.3 – Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (infrarenal)  

**Triage Data**  
Triage Cat: 1; Blood Pressure: 88/52 mmHg; Pulse: 124 /min; SpO₂: 96 %; Temp: 36.8 °C; VAS: 9; GCS: 15  

**Vital Signs**  

| Time (min) | BP (mmHg) | HR (bpm) | RR (breaths/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Temp (°C) |
|------------|-----------|----------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 0 (arrival) | 88/52 | 124 | 22 | 96 | 36.8 |
| 15 | 92/56 | 118 | 20 | 97 | 36.9 |
| 30 | 100/60 | 110 | 20 | 97 | 37.0 |
| 45 (pre‑CT) | 105/62 | 108 | 19 | 98 | 37.0 |

**Reason for Arrival**  
Sudden, severe “tearing” central abdominal pain radiating to the left flank with near‑syncope.

**Anamnesis**  

- **History:**  
  • Hypertension (12 y, on lisinopril 20 mg daily)  
  • Hyperlipidemia (on atorvastatin 40 mg nightly)  
  • Known infrarenal AAA 4.2 cm (screened 2 y ago)  
  • Chronic kidney disease stage 2 (eGFR ≈ 70 mL/min/1.73 m²)  
  • No prior surgeries  

- **Current Illness:**  
  0 h (≈ 30 min before arrival) – While gardening, experienced sudden sharp tearing pain in the mid‑abdomen radiating to the left flank, described as “being ripped apart.”  
  +5 min – Pain intensified, became constant; associated pressure sensation in the chest, light‑headedness, brief near‑syncope when standing.  
  +10 min – Abdomen felt hard; palpable pulsatile mass 2 cm above umbilicus; nausea without vomiting.  
  +15 min – Called EMS because pain persisted and blood pressure felt “very low.”  
  +20 min – EMS arrival: patient diaphoretic, pale, still in severe pain.  
  +30 min – Transported to ED; no prior similar episodes, no trauma, no recent heavy lifting.  

- **Functions:**  
  • Bowel: Normal last BM yesterday, no constipation/diarrhea.  
  • Urination: Adequate urine output reported, no dysuria.  

- **Habits:**  
  • Smoking: 45 pack‑years, quit 5 y ago.  
  • Alcohol: 1–2 standard drinks on weekends, no binge drinking.  

- **Medications:**  
  • Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily (held on arrival due to hypotension)  
  • Metoprolol ER 50 mg PO daily (held on arrival due to hypotension)  
  • Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly  
  • Aspirin 81 mg PO daily  
  • Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily  

- **Allergies:**  
  • Penicillin – urticarial rash (type I).  

**Status**  

- **General:** Pale, diaphoretic, anxious; supine with knees flexed for comfort.  
- **Cardiovascular:** Rapid radial pulse, weak peripheral pulses, no murmurs.  
- **Respiratory:** Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles.  
- **Abdomen:**  
  • Inspection – Distended, tense abdomen.  
  • Palpation – Firm, pulsatile mass palpable ~2 cm above umbilicus with a palpable thrill; severe diffuse midline tenderness, especially over the mass; no guarding or rebound (early rupture).  
  • Percussion – Tympanic centrally, dullness over left flank (retroperitoneal hematoma).  
- **Back:** Left flank tenderness, no step‑offs.  
- **Extremities:** Cool, slightly mottled skin; capillary refill > 3 s; peripheral pulses diminished.  
- **Neurologic:** Alert, oriented ×3, GCS 15, no focal deficits.  

**Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations**  

- **ECG:** Sinus tachycardia 124 bpm, no ST‑segment changes, no acute ischemia.  

- **Laboratory Results:**  

| Test | Result | Units | Reference |
|------|--------|-------|-----------|
| Hemoglobin | 11.2 | g/dL | 13.5‑17.5 |
| Hematocrit | 33 | % | 41‑53 |
| White blood cells | 14.0 | ×10⁹/L | 4‑11 |
| Platelets | 210 | ×10⁹/L | 150‑400 |
| Sodium | 138 | mmol/L | 135‑145 |
| Potassium | 4.3 | mmol/L | 3.5‑5.1 |
| Chloride | 102 | mmol/L | 98‑106 |
| BUN | 28 | mg/dL | 7‑20 |
| Creatinine | 1.4 | mg/dL | 0.6‑1.3 |
| eGFR | 68 | mL/min/1.73 m² | >60 |
| Lactate | 4.2 | mmol/L | 0.5‑2.2 |
| PT | 13.0 | sec | 11‑13.5 |
| INR | 1.0 | – | 0.8‑1.2 |
| aPTT | 32 | sec | 25‑35 |
| Troponin I | <0.01 | ng/mL | <0.04 |
| D‑dimer | 1,200 | ng/mL FEU | <500 |
| Type & crossmatch | 2 units PRBCs cross‑matched, O‑negative available | – | – |

- **Imaging:**  

  1. **Bedside FAST (abdominal):** Positive for free intra‑abdominal fluid in left paracolic gutter; heterogeneous echo‑dense fluid consistent with retroperitoneal hematoma.  
  2. **Portable AAA ultrasound:** Infrarenal aortic diameter ≈ 6.5 cm with irregular wall, focal outpouching, and periaortic fluid suggesting contained rupture.  
  3. **CT Angiography (arterial phase, performed after rapid IV access & permissive hypotension):**  
     - Infrarenal AAA maximal transverse diameter **6.8 cm**.  
     - Posterolateral crescent‑shaped hyperdense area of contrast extravasation → active rupture.  
     - Large retroperitoneal hematoma extending into left psoas muscle and left paracolic gutter.  
     - No involvement of renal arteries or visceral branches.  
  4. **Chest X‑ray (AP):** No mediastinal widening; lungs clear.  

- **Consults:** Vascular Surgery (Dr. Marko Horvat) activated emergently; Anesthesiology consulted for intra‑operative planning; ICU team notified.  

**Therapy Administered in ED**  

1. **Airway/O₂:** 2 L/min nasal cannula, SpO₂ maintained > 96 %.  
2. **IV Access:** Two 16‑gauge large‑bore peripheral lines placed; rapid infusion set attached.  
3. **Fluid Resuscitation:** 1 L balanced crystalloid (Plasma‑Lyte) administered over 15 min (permissive hypotension strategy).  
4. **Vasopressor:** Norepinephrine infusion started at 0.05 µg/kg/min; titrated to maintain SBP 80‑90 mmHg.  
5. **Tranexamic Acid:** 1 g IV loading dose over 10 min (per massive‑hemorrhage protocol) **followed by a continuous infusion of 1 g over 8 h**.  
6. **Antibiotic Prophylaxis:** Cefazolin 2 g IV administered (peri‑operative prophylaxis for vascular surgery).  
7. **Analgesia:** IV fentanyl 100 µg bolus, followed by IV ketamine 10 mg (low‑dose) for additional analgesia while preserving hemodynamics.  
8. **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4 mg IV.  
9. **Blood Products:** 1 unit PRBC transfused (type‑specific) started at 45 min; repeat hemoglobin after transfusion **12.0 g/dL**.  
10. **Medication Adjustments:** Lisinopril and Metoprolol were held on arrival due to hypotension.  
11. **Monitoring:** Continuous ECG, non‑invasive BP every 2 min, pulse oximetry, Foley catheter placed (output 30 mL/hr).  

**Conclusion**  

- **Clinical Summary:** 68‑year‑old male with known infrarenal AAA presented with classic “tearing” abdominal pain, hypotension, tachycardia, and a palpable pulsatile abdominal mass. Bedside ultrasound and CT angiography confirmed a ruptured infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm measuring 6.8 cm with active contrast extravasation and extensive retroperitoneal hematoma. The patient was hemodynamically unstable but responded to permissive hypotension, limited crystalloid, norepinephrine, early blood product administration, and appropriate adjunctive therapies.  

- **Diagnosis:** I71.3 – Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (infrarenal).  

- **Disposition:** Patient transferred emergently to the operating theatre for **endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR)** (preferred given anatomy and rapid availability). Post‑operative care planned in the surgical ICU with continued hemodynamic monitoring, repeat labs, and blood product support as needed.  

- **Recommendations:**  
  1. Maintain SBP 80‑90 mmHg until definitive aortic control.  
  2. Continue norepinephrine infusion intra‑operatively; consider additional vasopressor support if needed.  
  3. Repeat CBC, coagulation profile, and lactate after surgery.  
  4. Monitor renal function closely (baseline CKD stage 2).  
  5. Post‑operative CTA to assess endograft position and residual hematoma.  
  6. Continue peri‑operative antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., cefazolin) for 24 h post‑operatively.  

**Prepared by:** Dr. Ana Kovač, MD – Emergency Medicine Attending (ED)  
**Date of Report:** 2026‑02‑02  

**Vascular Surgery Consultant:** Dr. Marko Horvat, MD – Vascular Surgery
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1952

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
I48.9 Atrial fibrillation, unspecified
N39.0 Urinary tract infection, site not specified
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 138/88 mmHg; Pulse: 112/min (Irregular); SpO2: 96%; Temp: 37.8°C; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Date	BP (mmHg)	Pulse (min)	SpO2 (%)	Resp (min)	Temp (°C)	Blood Glucose (mmol/L)	Vas GCS AVPU
22.08.2024 10:15	135/85	110	97%	18	37.8	6.2	A
Reason for Arrival
Palpitations and urinary frequency.
Anamnesis
CURRENT ILLNESS: Patient presents complaining of rapid heart rate and "fluttering" sensation in the chest starting yesterday evening. Concurrently reports dysuria, urinary frequency, and foul-smelling urine for the past 3 days. Reports feeling feverish but did not measure temperature at home. No chest pain, no syncope, no shortness of breath.
Medical History: Permanent Atrial Fibrillation (anticoagulated), Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Hypertension, Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia.
Chronic Medication: Warfarin 3mg daily (INR target 2.0-3.0), Metformin 1000mg BID, Ramipril 5mg, Tamsulosin 0.4mg.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Anaphylaxis).
Habits: Ex-smoker (quit 10 years ago), denies alcohol.
Status
Patient is alert, oriented x3, slightly flushed.
Head/Neck: Mucous membranes dry. JVP not elevated.
Heart: Tachycardic, irregularly irregular rhythm, variable S1. No murmurs or rubs.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, bowel sounds present. Tenderness on deep palpation in the suprapubic region. No costovertebral angle tenderness.
Extremities: Warm, no edema, pulses palpable but irregular.
Neurology: No focal deficits.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-lead ECG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (RVR), average rate 115 bpm. No ST-segment deviation.
Urinalysis: Appearance cloudy; SG 1.020; pH 6.0; Leukocytes 3+; Nitrites Positive; Blood 1+; Protein trace.
HEMATOLOGY - BLOOD COUNT: WBC 13.2↗ (1e9/L); RBC 4.50 (1e12/L); Hb 135 (g/L); Hct 0.40 (L/L); Platelets 198 (1e9/L);
HEMATOLOGY - DIFFERENTIAL: NEU% 78.4 (%); LYM% 15.2 (%);
COAGULATION: PV-share 0.35; INR 2.4 (Therapeutic range); APTT 28.0 (s);
BIOCHEMISTRY: Glucose 7.8 (mmol/L); Urea 8.1 (mmol/L); Creatinine 98 (µmol/L); eGFR 72 (mL/min/1.73m2); Na 138 (mmol/L); K 4.1 (mmol/L); CRP 42↗ (mg/L); Troponin T < 14 (ng/L).
Radiographic Imaging
Chest X-ray PA View:
Cardiomegaly noted. Pulmonary vasculature is prominent but no frank pulmonary edema. No focal consolidation or pleural effusion.
Findings and Opinion
Patient presents with symptomatic Atrial Fibrillation with RVR, likely triggered by an underlying lower urinary tract infection as evidenced by urinalysis and inflammatory markers.
In the ED, patient received 1 Liter 0.9% Saline and Metoprolol 5mg IV push.
Re-evaluation at 13:00: Heart rate improved to 88-95 bpm (resting). Patient reports symptom relief. Blood pressure stable at 128/78 mmHg.
Infection requires antibiotic therapy. Due to documented severe Penicillin allergy, beta-lactams are contraindicated.

Conclusion
The clinical picture is consistent with a urinary tract infection exacerbating chronic atrial fibrillation. Rate control has been achieved in the department.
The patient is fit for discharge with home antibiotic therapy.
1. Prescription issued for Ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours for 7 days for the UTI.
2. Patient is instructed to continue all chronic medication, including Warfarin, at the usual dosage.
3. Increase fluid intake.
4. Follow up with GP in 3-5 days for re-evaluation of heart rate and resolution of urinary symptoms.
5. Return to ED if fever persists >48h, chest pain develops, or palpitations recur severely.
gold_reference: Ciprofloxacin (a fluoroquinolone) significantly interacts with Warfarin by inhibiting CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 metabolism and altering gut flora. This typically causes a dramatic increase in INR, placing the patient at high risk of severe bleeding. Discharging a patient on this combination without a plan to reduce the Warfarin dose or strictly monitor INR within 48 hours is a dangerous medical error.

• Blood Pressure (BP): 132/78 mmHg  
• Pulse: 108/min  
• SpO₂: 97% (room air)  
• Respirations: 18/min  
• Temperature: 38.6 °C (tympanic)  
• VAS: 6/10 (flank pain)  
• Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): 15  
________________________________________  
History  
Present Illness:  
• 2-day history of fever with chills, right-sided flank pain, nausea, and urinary frequency.  
• Burning with urination since yesterday; urine noted to be darker and foul-smelling.  
• No syncope, no chest pain, no shortness of breath.  

Medical History:  
• Type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin), generally well controlled (last known HbA1c ~7% per patient).  
• Recurrent kidney stones (last episode 2022, passed spontaneously).  
• No known chronic kidney disease.  

Surgical History:  
• Appendectomy (2005).  

Medications (home):  
• Metformin 1000 mg 2×1  
• Atorvastatin 20 mg 1×1  

Allergies:  
• Penicillin – anaphylaxis as a teenager (reported facial swelling, wheeze, treated in hospital).  

Habits:  
• Former smoker (quit 2019), denies alcohol and illicit drugs.  

Functions:  
• Decreased oral intake today due to nausea; normal bowel movement yesterday.  
________________________________________  
Physical Examination  
• General: Ill-appearing but alert, oriented, cooperative; febrile; mildly dehydrated (dry mucosa).  
• Skin: Warm, no rash.  
• Lungs: Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles/wheeze.  
• Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
• Abdomen: Soft, non-distended; mild suprapubic tenderness; no guarding or rebound.  
• Kidneys: Marked right costo-vertebral angle tenderness (CVA).  
• Extremities: No edema; capillary refill <2 s.  

ECG (16:02): Sinus tachycardia 105/min, no ischemic ST-T changes.  
________________________________________  
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests  

Point-of-care urine dip (15:40):  
• Leukocyte esterase: +++  
• Nitrites: +  
• Blood: ++  
• Protein: +  
• Ketones: trace  
• Glucose: + (consistent with diabetes)  

Laboratory Findings (reported 17:05):  
Hematology:  
• WBC: 14.8 ×10⁹/L (neutrophils 86%)  
• Hemoglobin: 143 g/L  
• Platelets: 262 ×10⁹/L  

Inflammation:  
• CRP: 86 mg/L  
• Procalcitonin: 0.62 ng/mL  

Renal / Metabolic:  
• Urea: 7.9 mmol/L  
• Creatinine: 104 µmol/L (eGFR ~78 mL/min/1.73 m²)  
• Glucose: 12.4 mmol/L  
• Na: 136 mmol/L  
• K: 4.2 mmol/L  
• Cl: 101 mmol/L  
• HCO₃: 22 mmol/L  

Liver enzymes:  
• AST: 18 U/L, ALT: 20 U/L, ALP: 78 U/L, bilirubin: 9 µmol/L  

Venous blood gas (17:10):  
• pH 7.39, lactate 1.6 mmol/L  

Microbiology:  
• Urine culture sent (pending).  
• Two sets of blood cultures drawn prior to antibiotics (pending).  

Imaging:  
Renal/urinary tract ultrasound (18:10):  
• Right kidney: normal size, mild pelvicalyceal fullness; no definite obstructing stone visualized.  
• Left kidney: unremarkable.  
• Bladder: moderate filling, no focal lesion.  
Impression: No definite hydronephrosis/obstruction; correlate clinically and consider CT if worsening or persistent obstruction suspected.  
________________________________________  
Therapy in ED  
• IV access, monitoring.  
• 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV over 90 minutes.  
• Paracetamol 1 g IV for fever/pain.  
• Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea.  
• Ceftriaxone 1 g IV administered at 18:00 after cultures were taken.  
________________________________________  
Clinical Course / Observation  
• After fluids and antipyretic, temperature decreased to 37.8 °C, HR improved to 92/min, BP stable at 126/76 mmHg.  
• Pain improved to VAS 3/10; tolerating oral fluids.  
• No signs of sepsis or shock; lactate normal, mental status normal.  
________________________________________  
Discharge Therapy / Prescriptions  
• Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg, 1 tablet orally every 12 hours for 10 days.  
• Paracetamol 500 mg tablets, 1–2 tablets orally every 6–8 hours as needed (max 3 g/day).  
• Continue home medications.  
________________________________________  
Conclusion  
Findings (fever, right flank pain, CVA tenderness, positive urine dip, leukocytosis and elevated CRP) are consistent with acute pyelonephritis. Ultrasound shows no clear obstruction. Patient improved with IV fluids, antipyretic, antiemetic, and initial IV antibiotic dose; hemodynamically stable and able to take oral intake.  

Recommendations / Follow-up:  
• Return immediately for worsening pain, persistent vomiting, inability to drink, confusion, fainting, reduced urine output, or persistent fever >48 hours.  
• Review urine/blood culture results with primary care physician within 48–72 hours (antibiotic adjustment as needed).  
• Consider non-contrast CT KUB if colicky pain develops or if no clinical improvement (rule out occult obstructing stone).  
• Diabetes sick-day advice and hydration emphasized.

gold_reference: The patient reports penicillin anaphylaxis. Amoxicillin/clavulanate is a penicillin-class antibiotic and is contraindicated due to risk of severe IgE-mediated reaction (including anaphylaxis).

report_content:
**VALIDATED AND CORRECTED ED REPORT**

**REPORT:**  
Date of Birth: 1981  

**Triage Category:** 2  

**Diagnoses**  
I26.99 Pulmonary embolism without acute cor pulmonale  
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension  

**Triage Data**  
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 132/78 mmHg; Pulse: 112/min; SpO₂: 91%; Temp: 37.2°C; VAS: 6; GCS: 15  

**Vital Signs**  

| Time (hh:mm) | BP (mmHg) | HR (bpm) | RR (breaths/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Temp (°C) |
|--------------|-----------|----------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 08:15 (triage) | 132/78 | 112 | 22 | 91 | 37.2 |
| 08:25 (post O₂ 2 L NC) | 130/76 | 108 | 20 | 95 | 37.2 |
| 08:45 (post enoxaparin) | 128/74 | 106 | 20 | 96 | 37.1 |

**Reason for Arrival**  
Sudden shortness of breath that started 2 hours ago while I was sitting on the couch.  

**Anamnesis**  
History: Hypertension diagnosed 5 years ago, controlled with lisinopril; no prior venous thromboembolism, malignancy, or known clotting disorder.  
Current Illness: Abrupt onset of dyspnea 2 h ago at rest, described as central chest tightness (severity 8/10 for breathlessness, 6/10 for chest discomfort). Associated pleuritic chest pain worsened by deep inspiration, light‑headedness, mild dizziness, and a single non‑productive cough; no hemoptysis. Symptoms have been constant since onset. Recent risk factors include a 14‑hour nonstop flight from London to Chicago 3 days ago (economy class, limited leg movement) and ongoing combined oral contraceptive use. No recent trauma or surgery.  
Functions: Normal bowel movements; no constipation or diarrhea. Normal urination; no dysuria or frequency.  
Habits: Never smoker; social alcohol use (1–2 glasses of wine on weekends); denies illicit drug use.  
Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily (hypertension); Ethinyl‑estradiol 35 µg / levonorgestrel 150 µg combined oral contraceptive 1 tablet PO daily (contraception); Acetaminophen 500 mg PO PRN for headache.  
Allergies: No known drug allergies (NKDA); no food or environmental allergies.  

**Status**  

- **General:** Alert, oriented ×3, mildly diaphoretic, appears anxious.  
- **Cardiovascular:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no jugular venous distension.  
- **Respiratory:** Clear to auscultation bilaterally, mild tachypnea, no wheezes or crackles.  
- **Extremities:** No peripheral edema; calf circumference symmetrical; no tenderness on deep palpation; Homan’s sign negative.  
- **Skin:** No cyanosis, no rashes.  
- **Neurologic:** Grossly intact, no focal deficits.  

**Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations**  

ECG: Sinus tachycardia 112 bpm; S1Q3T3 pattern (prominent S wave in lead I, Q wave and T‑wave inversion in lead III); right‑axis deviation.  

Laboratory Results:  
- **CBC:** Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL; Hematocrit 39 %; WBC 9.1 ×10⁹/L; Platelets 210 ×10⁹/L.  
- **BMP:** Na⁺ 138 mmol/L; K⁺ 4.2 mmol/L; Cl⁻ 102 mmol/L; BUN 12 mg/dL; Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; Glucose 92 mg/dL.  
- **ABG (room air):** pH 7.48; PaCO₂ 30 mm Hg; PaO₂ 68 mm Hg; HCO₃⁻ 22 mmol/L; Lactate 1.2 mmol/L.  
- **D‑dimer:** 1,800 ng/mL (ref < 500 ng/mL).  
- **Cardiac Troponin I:** 0.04 ng/mL (ref < 0.01 ng/mL).  
- **BNP:** 120 pg/mL (ref < 100 pg/mL).  
- **Coagulation:** PT 12 sec; INR 1.0; aPTT 30 sec.  
- **β‑hCG:** Negative.  

Imaging:  
- **Chest X‑ray (PA & lateral):** No infiltrates, effusions, or pneumothorax; clear lung fields; cardiac silhouette normal.  
- **CT Pulmonary Angiography:** Filling defect within segmental branches of the right lower‑lobe pulmonary artery extending into subsegmental branches; no aortic dissection or other thoracic pathology.  
- **Lower‑extremity venous duplex ultrasound:** Compressible deep veins in proximal femoral and popliteal segments; no DVT identified.  
- **Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram:** Right‑ventricular dilation with McConnell’s sign; estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure ≈ 45 mm Hg; left‑ventricular function normal.  

**Therapy Administered in ED**  
- Supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula 2 L/min (SpO₂ ↑ to 96%).  
- Enoxaparin 70 mg subcutaneously (1 mg/kg, q12 h) initiated – therapeutic anticoagulation.  
- Acetaminophen 650 mg PO for chest discomfort.  
- Two 18‑gauge peripheral IV lines placed.  
- Cardiac telemetry and continuous pulse‑ox monitoring.  
- Blood samples drawn for CBC, BMP, ABG, D‑dimer, troponin, BNP, coagulation panel, and β‑hCG.  
- CTPA performed with intravenous contrast.  
- Pulmonology consult obtained; recommendation for therapeutic anticoagulation and risk‑stratified management.  
- Patient education regarding cessation of estrogen‑containing contraception, importance of ambulation, hydration, and warning signs of clinical deterioration.  

**Risk Stratification**  
- Simplified PESI (sPESI) score: 1 point (HR ≥ 110 bpm). sPESI > 0 indicates intermediate‑risk.  
- ESC 2022 risk classification: Presence of right‑ventricular dysfunction on echo **and** positive cardiac biomarkers (troponin I 0.04 ng/mL, BNP 120 pg/mL) → **intermediate‑high‑risk** pulmonary embolism.  

**Conclusion**  
A 45‑year‑old woman presented with acute dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and hypoxemia. Elevated D‑dimer and CTPA confirmed a segmental pulmonary embolism in the right lower lobe. She remains hemodynamically stable (BP 132/78 mmHg) but demonstrates mild right‑ventricular strain (elevated troponin, BNP, echocardiographic findings), classifying her as **intermediate‑high‑risk PE** per ESC criteria. Immediate anticoagulation with low‑molecular‑weight heparin was started, with a plan to transition to a direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban) and to discontinue estrogen‑containing oral contraceptives.  

**Disposition**  
Admit to the observation unit for ≥ 24‑hour monitoring of anticoagulation efficacy, hemodynamic status, and potential clinical deterioration. If stable, transition to oral anticoagulation and discharge with follow‑up in hematology/vascular clinic and primary‑care physician. Recommendations include continuation of therapeutic anticoagulation for at least three months, reassessment of VTE risk factors, counseling on alternative contraception, and education on signs of recurrence.  

Signed: Dr. Ana Kovač, MD – Emergency Medicine Attending – 02/02/2026 09:30.

Reason for Arrival
Epigastric pain and nausea.
Anamnesis
History: Treated for arterial hypertension for 12 years and hyperlipidemia. Chronic GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) diagnosed 3 years ago via endoscopy. Appendectomy in youth.
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of "burning" pain in the epigastric region approximately 4 hours ago, shortly after a large meal. The pain is persistent, rated 6/10, and is accompanied by mild nausea. Denies vomiting, diarrhea, or fever. No radiation of pain to the back or shoulders. Patient took an over-the-counter antacid with minimal relief. 
Functions: Normal urination, last stool yesterday (normal consistency and color).
Habits: Former smoker (quit 2 years ago), social alcohol consumption.
Medications: Amlodipine 5 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg, Pantoprazole 20 mg p.r.n.
Allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build, overweight, oriented, communicative, eupneic, afebrile. Skin and mucous membranes appear normal. No peripheral edema. Neck veins not distended. 
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. 
Heart: Regular rhythm, heart sounds slightly muffled, no audible murmurs or gallops. BP 158/94 mmHg, pulse 94/min.
Abdomen: Soft, moderately tender on deep palpation in the epigastric region. No rebound tenderness or guarding. Liver and spleen not palpable. Audible peristalsis. Murphy's sign negative.
Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses present and symmetrical.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus rhythm, frequency 94/min, intermediate electrical axis. Non-specific ST-segment depressions (0.5 mm) in leads V5-V6. No T-wave inversion.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 9.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.75 [1e12]/L, HGB 151 g/L, Hct 0.442 L/L, Platelets 225 [1e9]/L, NEU 68%, LYM 22%, Glucose 7.8 mmol/L, Urea 6.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m², AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, GGT 41 U/L, CRP 3.2 mg/L, Na 140 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
hs-TNT (High-sensitivity Troponin T): 52.4 ng/L (Reference range: < 14.0 ng/L).
(Dr. Ana Šimić, Medical Biochemist, 22.08.2024 19:45)

Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (PA):
Findings and Opinion:
Heart shadow is of borderline size. Lungs are clear of infiltrates or congestion. Costophrenic angles are sharp. No free air under the diaphragm.
Dr. Goran Marković
Conclusion:
(Dr. sc. IVANA RADIĆ, MD, 22.08.2024 20:15)

Therapy Administered in ED:
- Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
- Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
- 500 ml 0.9% NaCl IV infusion

Conclusion
The patient's epigastric pain improved significantly following the administration of a proton pump inhibitor and antiemetic. Clinical presentation and abdominal tenderness are consistent with an acute exacerbation of gastritis/GERD. The patient is hemodynamically stable. 
Discharged home with instructions to continue Pantoprazole 40 mg once daily and maintain a light diet. Follow-up with primary care physician in 2-3 days. In case of worsening pain or vomiting, return to the emergency department.
gold_reference: The physician missed a diagnosis of Acute Coronary Syndrome (specifically NSTEMI). The report shows a significantly elevated high-sensitivity Troponin T (52.4 ng/L, which is nearly 4x the upper limit of normal) and non-specific ST-segment depressions on the ECG. Epigastric pain is a common 'atypical' presentation of myocardial ischemia, especially in patients with cardiovascular risk factors like hypertension and hyperlipidemia. The physician incorrectly attributed the improvement in pain to the GI medications (the 'GI cocktail' effect), which is a known cognitive pitfall.

gold_reference: The report identifies a patient with active chest pain, new ST-segment depression on ECG (V4-V6), and an elevated high-sensitivity Troponin T (52 ng/L). These findings are diagnostic of a Non-ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI). The clinician failed to order the necessary interventions: admission to a monitored bed (cardiology), initiation of anticoagulation (e.g., Heparin), and an urgent cardiology consultation for potential invasive management. Discharging an NSTEMI patient for outpatient follow-up carries a high risk of major adverse cardiac events.

Vital Signs
Date: 18.08.2024 09:15; BP: 148/92; Pulse: 122; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.7; GUK: 8.4 mmol/L
Anamnesis
Personal Anamnesis: The patient has a long-standing history of arterial hypertension and Type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with oral hypoglycemics. Known history of compensated heart failure (EF 45% at last check-up 6 months ago). No previous history of stroke or TIA.
Current Illness: Patient reports a sudden onset of "racing heart" and "fluttering" in the chest starting this morning around 05:00. The palpitations are accompanied by mild shortness of breath and a feeling of lightheadedness when standing. Denies chest pain, syncope, or focal neurological deficits. She has not experienced similar episodes in the past. 
Functions: Urination normal, bowel movements regular. Appetite slightly decreased today due to anxiety.
Habits: Non-smoker, does not consume alcohol.
Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg 1x1, Metformin 850 mg 2x1, Furosemide 20 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
Elderly female, alert, oriented x3, appears anxious but in no acute respiratory distress.
Skin: Warm, dry, no cyanosis or jaundice.
Neck: No JVD noted at 45 degrees.
Lungs: Mild bibasilar crackles noted on auscultation, no wheezing.
Heart: Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic, S1/S2 vary in intensity, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, bowel sounds present, no organomegaly.
Extremities: 1+ pitting edema bilaterally to the ankles. Symmetrical pulses, though irregular.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
EKG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, heart rate 124/min. No ST-segment elevation or T-wave inversion. Normal axis. QRS duration 92ms.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 7.2 [1e9]/L, Hb 134 g/L, Hct 0.401 L/L, Trc 245 [1e9]/L, Glucose 8.4 mmol/L, Urea 7.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 102 µmol/L, eGFR 54 mL/min/1.73m², AST 24 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, GGT 35 U/L, CRP 4.2 mg/L, Troponin T (hs) 12 ng/L (normal <14), NT-proBNP 890 pg/mL, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
(PETROVIĆ ANA, mag.med.biochem., 18.08.2024 10:45)
Chest X-ray (AP):
Mild prominence of the pulmonary vasculature. Borderline cardiomegaly. No acute consolidations or large pleural effusions.
(Dr. M. Horvat, 18.08.2024 11:05)
Therapy
    • Metoprolol 2.5 mg iv (administered twice)
    • Pantoprazole 40 mg iv
    • Diazepam 5 mg po (for anxiety)
Conclusion
(Dr. MARIĆ IVAN dr.med., 18.08.2024 13:30)
The patient presented with a first-detected episode of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Following the administration of intravenous beta-blockers, the heart rate was successfully controlled to 85/min, and the patient spontaneously converted to sinus rhythm as confirmed by a follow-up EKG at 13:00. 
Risk assessment for thromboembolism was performed. CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 1 point (female sex). Based on this low risk score, chronic oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time. The patient is stable for discharge.
Recommendations:
1. Increase Lisinopril to 20 mg daily.
2. Start Bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1x1.
3. Acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin) 100 mg daily for general cardiovascular prophylaxis.
4. Follow-up with a cardiologist and 24-hour Holter monitoring within 2 weeks.

gold_reference: The patient is presenting with classic Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA). The labs show severe hyperglycemia (28.4 mmol/L), metabolic acidosis (pH 7.22, HCO3 11.2), and significant ketosis (Beta-Hydroxybutyrate 4.2, Urine Ketones 3+). ________________________________________
Reason for Visit
Severe pain and swelling in the right big toe.
________________________________________
Medical History (Anamnesis)
Current Complaints: Patient presents with acute onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling in the right first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint. Symptoms began overnight, waking the patient from sleep.
•	Describes pain as "throbbing" and "burning," rated 8/10.
•	Unable to bear weight on the right foot. Even the weight of the bedsheet causes pain.
•	Reports attending a barbecue yesterday with alcohol consumption and red meat intake.
•	Denies trauma to the foot.
Medical History:
•	Hypertension (long-standing).
•	Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (diet controlled).
•	Chronic Kidney Disease (Stage 4), under nephrology follow-up. Last known creatinine ~220 µmol/L.
•	Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia.
Medications:
•	Ramipril 5 mg daily.
•	Amlodipine 10 mg daily.
•	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily.
•	Atorvastatin 20 mg daily.
Drug Allergies:
•	Penicillin (Rash).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General: Alert, oriented, in visible distress due to pain. Slightly flushed face.
•	Cardiovascular: Heart sounds S1, S2 regular. No murmurs. BP elevated (pain response).
•	Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Eupneic.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, bowel sounds present.
•	Extremities:
    o	Left leg: Unremarkable.
    o	Right leg: Significant erythema, warmth, and edema localized to the 1st MTP joint. Extreme tenderness to palpation. Range of motion limited by pain. No signs of ascending lymphangitis. Peripheral pulses palpable (dorsalis pedis pulse strong).
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Sinus rhythm, rate 88 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy criteria met (voltage). No acute ST-T wave changes.
Laboratory Results:
Hematology:
•	WBC: 12.1 ↗ (10^9/L) (Mild leukocytosis)
•	RBC: 4.05 (10^12/L)
•	Hb: 128 (g/L)
•	Plt: 245 (10^9/L)

•	Glucose: 6.8 mmol/L
•	Urea: 18.2 ↗ mmol/L
•	Creatinine: 235 ↗ µmol/L
•	eGFR: 24 ↘ mL/min/1.73m² (Consistent with CKD Stage 4)
•	Uric Acid: 540 ↗ µmol/L
•	CRP: 18.5 ↗ mg/L
•	Electrolytes: Na 139, K 4.8, Cl 104.
Imaging:
X-ray Right Foot (AP/Oblique):
•	Significant soft tissue swelling around the 1st MTP joint.
•	No acute fracture or dislocation.
•	Small "punched-out" erosions visible at the joint margins consistent with chronic gouty arthropathy.

•	Diclofenac (Voltaren) 75 mg i.m. administered at 09:00 for immediate pain relief.
•	Ice pack applied to the area.
________________________________________
Conclusion
•	Clinical presentation and laboratory findings confirm acute gouty arthritis (Podagra).
•	Infection (septic arthritis) unlikely given the history, afebrile status, and classic presentation, though CRP and WBC are mildly elevated due to inflammation.
•	No fractures noted.

Recommendations:
•	Discharge to home care.
•	Rest and elevation of the foot.
•	Prescription: Ibuprofen 600 mg orally every 8 hours as needed for pain and inflammation for the next 5 days.
•	Dietary advice: Avoid purine-rich foods (red meat, shellfish) and alcohol.
•	Follow up with GP in 7 days for renal function check and to discuss starting urate-lowering therapy (Allopurinol) once the acute attack has resolved.
•	Return to ED if fever develops or pain becomes unmanageable.
gold_reference: The patient has Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease with an eGFR of 24 mL/min/1.73m². Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like Diclofenac and Ibuprofen are contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment (typically eGFR < 30) because they inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, causing afferent arteriole constriction, which can precipitate Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) and worsen the existing CKD.

Date of Birth: 1988

Triage Category: 3  
Diagnoses  
N20.1 Calculus of ureter (right distal ureterolithiasis)  
R10.9 Unspecified abdominal pain (flank pain)

---

## Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 148/92 mmHg; Pulse: 102/min; SpO2: 99%; Resp: 18/min; Temp: 36.7°C; VAS: 8 – Severe pain; GCS: 15

## Vital Signs
Date | BP /mmHg | Pulse /min | SpO2 % | Resp /min | Temp °C | VAS | GCS
---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:
06.02.2026 09:14 | 148/92 | 102 | 99 | 18 | 36.7 | 8 | 15
06.02.2026 10:05 | 132/84 | 88 | 99 | 16 | 36.6 | 3 | 15

---

## Reason for Visit
Acute right-sided flank pain

---

## History
**CURRENT ILLNESS:** Patient reports sudden onset of severe right flank pain starting ~2 hours prior to arrival, described as colicky, waxing/waning, radiating toward the right groin. Associated nausea without vomiting. He notes urinary urgency and mild dysuria since this morning. Denies fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, or trauma. No testicular swelling; pain is not positional and does not worsen with respiration.

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:** Prior kidney stone ~5 years ago (passed spontaneously). No known diabetes. No known chronic kidney disease.

**MEDICATIONS:** None regularly.

**ALLERGIES:** No known drug allergies.

**SOCIAL HISTORY:** Occasional alcohol; non-smoker.

**FUNCTIONS:** Last bowel movement yesterday, normal. No gross blood in stool. Urination today frequent in small amounts.

---

## Status (Physical Examination)
Medium build, in visible discomfort, intermittently restless due to pain. Oriented x3, GCS 15. Skin warm, well perfused, no rash.

- **HEENT:** Mucosa moist, no icterus.
- **Chest/Lungs:** Symmetric chest movement; clear breath sounds bilaterally.
- **Heart:** Regular rhythm, no murmurs; peripheral pulses palpable.
- **Abdomen:** Soft, non-distended. Mild right lower quadrant tenderness without rebound/guarding. No palpable masses.  
- **CVA tenderness:** Marked tenderness over right costovertebral angle; left side non-tender.
- **GU:** No scrotal swelling or focal testicular tenderness reported; exam deferred by patient, no red-flag symptoms suggesting torsion.
- **Extremities:** No edema, no focal neurologic deficit.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations

### ECG
Sinus tachycardia ~102/min. No ischemic ST-T changes.

### Laboratory Results

**HEMATOLOGY – BLOOD COUNT:**  
WBC 10.9 [10⁹/L]; RBC 5.02 [10¹²/L]; Hb 151 g/L; Hct 0.447 L/L; MCV 89.0 fL; MCH 30.1 pg; MCHC 338 g/L; RDW-CV 13.1%; Platelets 242 [10⁹/L]; MPV 10.2 fL

**HEMATOLOGY – DIFFERENTIAL:**  
NEU% 78.0%; LYM% 15.3%; MONO% 5.8%; EOS% 0.6%; BASO% 0.3%  
NEU # 8.50 [10⁹/L]; LYM # 1.67 [10⁹/L]; MONO # 0.63 [10⁹/L]; EOS # 0.07 [10⁹/L]; BASO # 0.03 [10⁹/L]

**METABOLITES AND SUBSTRATES:**  
Glucose 5.6 mmol/L; Urea 6.4 mmol/L; Creatinine 86 µmol/L; eGFR 109 mL/min/1.73m²

**INFLAMMATION / PROTEINS:**  
CRP 3.2 mg/L

**ELECTROLYTES:**  
Na 140 mmol/L; K 4.3 mmol/L; Cl 104 mmol/L; Ca 2.30 mmol/L

**URINALYSIS (dipstick + sediment):**  
- Color: yellow; clarity: slightly cloudy  
- Specific gravity 1.020; pH 6.0  
- Protein: trace; Glucose: negative; Ketones: negative  
- Nitrite: negative; Leukocyte esterase: negative  
- Blood: 3+  
**Microscopy:** RBC 50–100 /HPF; WBC 0–3 /HPF; bacteria not seen; crystals: occasional calcium oxalate  
(Findings consistent with hematuria without evidence of urinary tract infection.)

### Imaging
**Non-contrast CT KUB (kidneys/ureters/bladder):**  
- 4 mm calculus in the **right distal ureter** near the ureterovesical junction.  
- Mild right hydroureter and mild hydronephrosis.  
- No perinephric stranding.  
- No additional stones causing obstruction.  
**Impression:** Right distal ureterolithiasis with mild upstream dilatation.

---

## Therapy in ED
- IV access, monitoring.
- **Analgesia:** Ketorolac 30 mg IV once.  
- **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4 mg IV once.  
- **Hydration:** 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV.

**Clinical response:** Pain decreased from VAS 8/10 to 3/10 within ~45 minutes; nausea resolved. Hemodynamically stable; afebrile.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture, urinalysis, and CT are consistent with **right distal ureteral stone (4 mm)** with mild hydronephrosis. There are **no signs of sepsis, pyelonephritis, acute kidney injury, or uncontrolled pain** after treatment; therefore, **no indication for emergency hospitalization** at this time.

### Discharge Plan
- **Medications:**
  - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (take with food; avoid exceeding recommended daily dose).
  - Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily for up to 14 days (medical expulsive therapy).
  - Ondansetron 4 mg PO as needed for nausea.
- **Instructions:**
  - Maintain oral hydration; strain urine to catch stone for analysis.
  - Arrange **urology follow-up** within 1–2 weeks (earlier if symptoms persist).
- **Return immediately / call EMS if:** fever ≥ 38°C, rigors, worsening flank pain not controlled with medication, inability to urinate, persistent vomiting/dehydration, fainting, or any new severe symptoms.

Patient discharged ambulatory, in improved condition, with verbal and written instructions.
