Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
I48.0 Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 158/92 mmHg; Pulse: 114/min; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.7°C; VAS: 2 - Mild discomfort; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Palpitations and mild shortness of breath.
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing essential hypertension treated for 15 years. No history of coronary artery disease, diabetes, or prior stroke. This is the first episode of palpitations.
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of "racing heart" and "fluttering" in the chest starting approximately 4 hours ago while resting. Accompanied by mild shortness of breath upon walking. Denies chest pain, syncope, or focal neurological deficits.
Functions and Habits: Normal bowel movements and urination. Non-smoker, does not consume alcohol. Appetite is normal.
Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Amlodipine 5 mg daily.
Allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).
Status
Patient is an elderly female of medium build, fully conscious, communicative, and oriented to person, place, and time. Eupneic at rest, afebrile, non-icteric, skin and mucous membranes are well-perfused.
Chest: Symmetrical respiratory movements.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Irregular rhythm, tachycardic, heart sounds are distinct, no murmurs or gallops. Peripheral pulses are irregular and palpable.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses or organomegaly. Peristalsis is present.
Extremities: No pretibial edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 112/min. Normal electrical axis. No ST-segment elevation or depression. No pathological Q waves.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.25 [1e12]/L, HGB 132 g/L, Hct 0.395 L/L, MCV 93.0 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, NEU 62%, LYM 28%.
Sodium 139 mmol/L, Potassium 4.1 mmol/L, Chloride 102 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m², Glucose 5.4 mmol/L.
Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL (Negative).
TSH 1.45 mIU/L (Normal).
(Dr. Elena Vukas, Clinical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 14:15)
Conclusion
The patient presents with a first-time episode of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Symptoms were managed in the ED with a single dose of Metoprolol 25 mg orally, resulting in a reduction of heart rate to 82/min (still irregular).
Regarding stroke prophylaxis, the CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 1 (1 point for Hypertension). Based on this score, the risk of thromboembolism is low, and oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time.
The patient is discharged with a prescription for Aspirin 100 mg daily and Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily.
Urgent follow-up with a cardiologist is recommended within 7 days for consideration of cardioversion or further rhythm management.
Report to the emergency department immediately if experiencing chest pain, severe shortness of breath, or signs of stroke (facial drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty).
(Dr. Marko Horvat, MD, 18.07.2024 16:30)
gold_reference: The physician miscalculated the CHA2DS2-VASc score. The patient is a 76-year-old female (Age >= 75 = 2 points; Female sex = 1 point) with Hypertension (1 point). The correct CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4, not 1. A score of 4 carries a high risk of stroke and necessitates oral anticoagulation (like Apixaban or Warfarin). Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation.

•	Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing.
•	Right Upper Extremity (Local Status):
    o	Two deep puncture wounds noted on the dorsal aspect of the right hand over the 1st dorsal interosseous muscle and thenar eminence.
    o	Wounds are approximately 0.5 - 1.0 cm deep with jagged edges; minimal active oozing.
    o	Surrounding tissue is mildly edematous and erythematous.
    o	Range of Motion: Full flexion and extension of all digits and wrist. Opposition of thumb intact.
    o	Neurovascular: Radial and ulnar pulses palpable (2+). Capillary refill <2 seconds in all digits. Sensation intact to light touch in median, ulnar, and radial distributions. Two-point discrimination normal at fingertips.
    o	No exposed tendon or bone visible.

•	No radio-opaque foreign bodies identified.

•	Wound Copiously irrigated with 500mL normal saline under pressure.

•	Patient treated for Grade II dog bite to the right hand.
•	Imaging ruled out fracture and foreign body.

gold_reference: The report describes deep puncture wounds from a dog bite to the hand. This is a high-risk injury for infection (Pasteurella multocida, etc.). Standard of care requires prophylactic antibiotics (typically Amoxicillin-Clavulanate) and an assessment/update of Tetanus immunization status. Neither was ordered.

**Emergency Department Report**

**Date of Birth:** 1958 (Age 67)  
**Sex:** Male

---

## Triage
**Triage Category:** 2 (Urgent)

### Diagnoses (ED)
- **K92.1 Melena**
- **K92.2 Gastrointestinal hemorrhage, unspecified**
- **R10.13 Epigastric pain**

### Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: **94/58 mmHg**; Pulse: **112/min**; SpO₂: **98% (room air)**; Resp: **18/min**; Temp: **36.7°C**; VAS: **5/10**; GCS: **15**

**Reason for Arrival:** Black stools since yesterday, dizziness on standing, epigastric pain.

---

## Anamnesis
**History of Present Illness:**  
Patient reports **two episodes of black, tarry stool** since last evening and **progressive dizziness** when standing. Describes **burning epigastric pain** for ~1 week, worse at night and after coffee. Denies hematemesis. Reports mild exertional dyspnea today but no chest pain. No syncope.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Coronary artery disease (PCI with stent 2016)  
- Hypertension  
- GERD (intermittent)

**Medications (home):**  
- Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg daily  
- Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily  
- Ramipril 5 mg daily  
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly  
- Occasional OTC antacids; denies regular PPI use

**Allergies:** No known drug allergies.

**Habits:** Former smoker (quit 10 years ago), alcohol rarely.

**Functional/Other:** Urination normal. No recent travel. No known liver disease. No anticoagulant use reported.

---

## Status (Physical Examination)
**General:** Pale, oriented, cooperative, mildly anxious.  
**Skin/Mucosa:** Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice.  
**Airway:** Patent.  
**Breathing:** Eupneic at rest, clear breath sounds bilaterally.  
**Circulation:** Tachycardic, cool extremities, capillary refill ~3 sec.  
**Heart:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, mild epigastric tenderness, no guarding/rebound. No organomegaly.  
**Rectal exam:** Black stool on glove, consistent with melena.  
**Extremities:** No edema. Peripheral pulses palpable but weak.  
**Neuro:** No focal deficits.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG
Sinus tachycardia ~110/min, no acute ischemic ST-T changes.

### Laboratory Results
**CBC:**
- WBC **9.6** [1e9]/L  
- RBC **3.42** [1e12]/L ↓  
- HGB **96 g/L** ↓  
- Hct **0.292 L/L** ↓  
- MCV **85.4 fL**  
- Platelets **238** [1e9]/L  

**Coagulation:**
- INR **1.04**  
- APTT **25.6 s**

**Biochemistry:**
- Urea **14.8 mmol/L** ↑  
- Creatinine **98 µmol/L** (eGFR **78 mL/min/1.73m²**)  
- Na **137 mmol/L**, K **4.4 mmol/L**  
- AST **24 U/L**, ALT **22 U/L**  
- CRP **3.1 mg/L**  
- Glucose **6.7 mmol/L**

**Type & Screen:** Blood group **A Rh+**, antibody screen negative.

*(Lab timestamp: 06.02.2026 13:22)*

### Imaging
**Bedside abdominal ultrasound (FAST/limited):** No free intraperitoneal fluid. Aorta normal caliber.  
**Chest X-ray (PA):** No acute cardiopulmonary pathology; no consolidation or pleural effusion.

---

## ED Course / Monitoring
- Two large-bore IV lines placed.
- Patient remained hemodynamically borderline (SBP 90–100 mmHg) with persistent tachycardia 105–115/min.
- Repeat vitals after initial fluids: BP **102/64**, HR **104**, SpO₂ **99%**.

---

## Therapy (ED)
- **0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV bolus**, then maintenance infusion  
- **Pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus**, then **8 mg/h IV infusion**  
- **Ondansetron 4 mg IV** (for nausea prophylaxis)  
- **Hold acetylsalicylic acid** pending gastroenterology decision  
- Crossmatch prepared; transfusion threshold discussed (symptoms + Hb 96 g/L; to reassess with ongoing bleeding)

**Consultations:** Gastroenterology contacted—recommend admission and endoscopy within 24 hours (earlier if instability/ongoing bleeding).

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture consistent with **upper GI bleeding (melena + urea elevation + anemia + epigastric pain)**, likely peptic source. Patient requires **inpatient management**, monitoring, serial hemoglobin, and **upper endoscopy (EGD)**.

**Admitted to Internal Medicine/Gastroenterology** for observation, IV PPI therapy, and urgent endoscopic evaluation. Return precautions explained.

**Discharge/ward medication instruction (written):**  
- **Ibuprofen 600 mg orally three times daily as needed for abdominal pain.**  ← **(ERROR)**

*(Physician: Dr. M. Example, 06.02.2026 14:05)*

---
gold_reference: Ibuprofen (an NSAID) is contraindicated in suspected/confirmed gastrointestinal bleeding and likely peptic ulcer disease because it increases the risk of further mucosal injury and recurrent/worsening bleeding. The patient already has melena and anemia, and is also on aspirin, which further increases bleeding risk.

Vital Signs
Anamnesis
History: Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia, Smoker (30 pack-years). No prior surgeries.
Current Illness: Patient presented to the ER complaining of retrosternal chest pressure starting approximately 2 hours ago while gardening. Describes the pain as "squeezing," radiating to the left shoulder and jaw. Associated with mild nausea and diaphoresis. Reports transient relief with rest, but pain recurred.
Medications: Amlodipine 5mg, Atorvastatin 20mg.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
From status: Mobile, of pyknic build, alert, afebrile, eupneic, slightly diaphoretic.
Lungs: Vesicular breathing sounds, basal crackles bilaterally, no wheezing.
Heart: Regular rhythm, tachycardia, S4 gallop audible. No murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, liver and spleen not palpable.
Extremities: No lower limb edema, peripheral pulses palpable but weak.
ECG: Sinus rhythm, rate 88/min, horizontal ST-segment depression of 1mm in leads V4-V6. T-wave inversion in aVL.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Chest X-ray, PA and Lateral:
Lung fields clear of focal consolidation. Mild pulmonary venous congestion noted. Cardiac silhouette borderline enlarged. Aortic knob calcified.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 24.10.2024 10:15)
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.5 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.8 [1e12]/L, HGB 145 g/L, Hct 0.42 L/L, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, Glucose 7.8 mmol/L, Urea 6.5 mmol/L, Creatinine 95 µmol/L, eGFR 72 mL/min, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, CRP 3.2 mg/L.
Troponin T hs: 28 ng/L (Ref: <14 ng/L).
CK-MB: 5.2 ng/mL.
D-Dimer: 0.35 mg/L (Negative).
(Dr. Marija Kovač, 24.10.2024 10:45)
Therapy
Aspirin 300 mg PO
Nitroglycerin spray s.l. x 2
Diazepam 5mg PO
Pantoprazole 40mg IV
Conclusion
The patient reports significant improvement in pain intensity (VAS 2/10) following sublingual nitrates and rest. ECG changes are non-specific and likely chronic given the history of hypertension. Although Troponin T is mildly elevated (28 ng/L), this is interpreted as a result of chronic hypertensive strain rather than acute coronary syndrome, given the pain resolution. Vital signs have normalized (BP 140/85 mmHg). No signs of pulmonary embolism.
Patient is discharged home. Advised to rest, continue chronic therapy, and visit primary care physician tomorrow for a follow-up ECG. Return to ED if pain becomes unbearable.
gold_reference: The patient presents with classic symptoms of Unstable Angina / NSTEMI (chest pain radiating to jaw/arm, diaphoresis), ischemic ECG changes (ST depression), and an elevated high-sensitivity Troponin (28 ng/L vs ref <14). Discharging a patient with positive cardiac biomarkers and active ischemic ECG changes is dangerous malpractice. Pain relief with nitrates does not rule out MI.

Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 145/88 mmHg; Pulse: 92/min; SpO2: 98%; Temp: 37.8°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Visit
Severe abdominal pain in the upper right quadrant and vomiting.
Anamnesis
History: The patient has a history of dyslipidemia controlled with statins. She underwent an appendectomy in 1995 and a Cesarean section in 2008.
Current Illness: The patient presents to the Emergency Department complaining of intense pain in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen, radiating to the right shoulder blade. The pain began approximately 8 hours ago, shortly after eating a heavy meal. She reports three episodes of vomiting biliary content since the onset of pain. She feels feverish and chills. She denies urinary symptoms or changes in stool color.
Functions: Last bowel movement yesterday, normal consistency. Urination normal.
Habits: Non-smoker. Drinks alcohol socially (1-2 glasses of wine per week).
Chronic Medications: Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (Anaphylaxis).
Status
Patient is of hypersthenic build, conscious, fully oriented, anxious due to pain. Skin is warm, slightly flushed, anicteric. Mucous membranes are slightly dry.
Head and Neck: Pupils equal and reactive to light. Throat calm. Neck supple, no lymphadenopathy.
Chest: Symmetrical respiratory excursion.
Lungs: Vesicular breathing sounds, clear bilaterally, no crackles or wheezes.
Heart: Regular rhythm, tachycardia (92/min), clear heart sounds, no murmurs. BP 145/85 mmHg.
Abdomen: Abdominal wall at chest level. Soft, but presents with distinct voluntary guarding in the right upper quadrant. Positive Murphy's sign (arrest of inspiration upon deep palpation of the gallbladder fossa). No generalized rebound tenderness. Peristalsis is audible but sluggish. Renal percussion painless bilaterally.
Extremities: No edema. Peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric. Capillary refill <2 sec.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
ECG: Sinus rhythm, 90/min, normal axis, no acute ST-T changes.
Abdominal Ultrasound: The liver is of normal size and homogenous echotexture. The gallbladder is distended with a thickened wall (4.5 mm) and contains multiple hyperechoic structures with acoustic shadowing (cholelithiasis). A small amount of pericholecystic fluid is noted. Positive sonographic Murphy's sign. Common bile duct is not dilated (5 mm). Pancreas and spleen appear normal. Kidneys show no hydronephrosis.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 14.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.5 [1e12]/L, HGB 132 g/L, PLT 240 [1e9]/L
NEU% 82.0 %, LYM% 12.0 %
CRP 45.2 mg/L
Glucose 6.1 mmol/L, Urea 4.5 mmol/L, Creatinine 70 µmol/L
AST 35 U/L, ALT 42 U/L, GGT 65 U/L (mild elevation), ALP 90 U/L, Bilirubin Total 18 µmol/L
Amylase 55 U/L, Lipase 40 U/L (Normal)
Urinalysis: Unremarkable.
Therapy
0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV
Paracetamol 1000 mg IV
Ketoprofen 100 mg IV in 100 ml 0.9% NaCl
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV (due to Penicillin allergy)
Metronidazole 500 mg IV
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Conclusion
Based on clinical presentation, laboratory findings (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and ultrasound confirmation, the patient is diagnosed with acute calculous cholecystitis.
Consultation with the abdominal surgeon on duty was performed.
Indicated for admission to the Department of Abdominal Surgery for hydration, antibiotic therapy, and consideration of laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Patient is kept NPO (Nil per os).

Medical History: History of Gout and Hyperlipidemia. Previous episode of nephrolithiasis (right side) 6 years ago, passed spontaneously.
Bowel movements regular.
Smoker (10 cigarettes/day), social alcohol use.
Chronic medication: Allopurinol 300 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
Patient appears in acute distress, unable to find a comfortable position (restless).
Skin: Pale, diaphoretic, warm.
Head/Neck: Mucous membranes slightly dry.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Bowel sounds present but hypoactive. Tenderness in the left lower quadrant on deep palpation, no rebound tenderness.
Back: Positive Goldflam sign (percussion tenderness) over the left kidney. Right side negative.
Extremities: No edema.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-lead ECG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 108/min, normal axis, no ST-T changes.
Abdominal Ultrasound (Focused): Left kidney shows moderate hydronephrosis. Right kidney normal size and echotexture. Bladder effectively empty.
CT Abdomen/Pelvis (Native/Low Dose):
Findings: A hyperdense calculus measuring 5 mm is visualized in the distal left ureter (ureterovesical junction). Proximal ureter dilated (8 mm). Left renal pelvis dilated. No perirenal stranding. Right urinary tract unremarkable. Appendix normal. No free air or fluid.

HEMATOLOGY - BLOOD COUNT: WBC 12.1↗ (1e9/L); RBC 5.10 (1e12/L); Hb 152 (g/L); Hct 0.450 (L/L); MCV 88.0 (fL); Platelets 280 (1e9/L);
HEMATOLOGY - DIFFERENTIAL: NEU% 78.2↗ (%); LYM% 15.1↘ (%); MONO% 5.0 (%);
COAGULATION: INR 1.01; APTT 24.0 (s);
METABOLITES AND SUBSTRATES: Blood Glucose 6.1 (mmol/L); Urea 7.2 (mmol/L); Creatinine 108↗ (µmol/L); eGFR 78 (mL/min/1.73m2); Uric Acid 410↗ (µmol/L); CRP 4.2 (mg/L);
ELECTROLYTES: Sodium 141 (mmol/L); Potassium 4.2 (mmol/L); Chloride 102 (mmol/L); Calcium 2.40 (mmol/L);
URINALYSIS: Specific Gravity 1.025; pH 6.0; Leukocytes 1+; Nitrites Negative; Protein Negative; Blood 3+; Ketones Negative.
SEDIMENT: Erythrocytes: Numerous/HPF; Leukocytes: 5-10/HPF; Bacteria: None.
Therapy
    • 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion
    • Diclofenac 75 mg IM
    • Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
    • Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO (given in observation)
Conclusion
Patient presented with clinical picture of acute renal colic.
CT confirms 5mm distal ureteral stone on the left with associated hydronephrosis.
Laboratory results show mild leukocytosis (likely stress-reactive) and mild elevation in creatinine (likely pre-renal/obstruction related). Urinalysis confirms hematuria but no signs of active UTI (Nitrites negative, CRP low).
Patient reports significant pain relief after analgesia (VAS 9 -> 2).
Discharged home with conservative management.
Therapy prescribed:
1. Diclofenac Duo 75 mg 1x1 daily as needed for pain.
2. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg 1x1 daily for 10 days (expulsive therapy).
3. Pantoprazole 20 mg 1x1.
Hydration recommended (2-3L fluid daily). Strain urine to catch stone for analysis.
Urology outpatient follow-up in 7-10 days or sooner if fever (>38°C), intractable pain, or inability to pass urine occurs.
Verbal instructions given.
gold_reference: No errors

Patient presents with a 4-day history of productive cough producing thick, rust-colored sputum. Reports high fevers starting yesterday with rigors. Complains of sharp, pleuritic chest pain on the left side, worsened by coughing and deep inspiration. Reports shortness of breath upon minimal exertion (walking to the bathroom).

Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Rash)

Patient appears fatigued, flushed, and dyspneic. Conscious, fully oriented to time, place, and person (GCS 15). Mucous membranes dry. Capillary refill time 3 seconds.
Head and Neck: No jugular venous distension. Pharynx slightly hyperemic, no exudates.
Lungs: Tachypnea. On auscultation, bronchial breathing and coarse inspiratory crackles heard over the left lower zone. Dullness to percussion over the left base. Right lung field clear.

NEU% 85.0 %, LYM% 10.0 %

Levofloxacin 500mg IV (due to Penicillin allergy).

Community-acquired pneumonia (left lower lobe).

gold_reference: The physician incorrectly calculated the CURB-65 score as 1. The patient has 3 points: Age 72 (>65) = 1 point; Urea 8.2 mmol/L (>7 mmol/L) = 1 point; Blood Pressure 88/55 mmHg (SBP <90 or DBP <60) = 1 point. A score of 3 indicates severe pneumonia with high mortality risk.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1956

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
E87.5 Hyperkalemia
N18.4 Chronic Kidney Disease, Stage 4
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 105/65 mmHg; Pulse: 48/min; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.2°C; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Date	RR (mmHg)	Pulse (min)	SpO2 (%)	Resp (min)	Temp (°C)
10.02.2024 09:15	108/68	49	95	18	36.2
10.02.2024 09:45	105/65	48	96	18	-
Reason for Visit
General weakness, nausea, and sensation of "skipping" heartbeats.
Anamnesis
Patient is a 68-year-old male with a known history of chronic kidney disease (Stage 4), Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, and arterial hypertension. He is followed by nephrology but is not yet on dialysis.
Current Illness: The patient presents to the ER complaining of profound generalized weakness that began yesterday evening and has worsened this morning. He reports difficulty lifting his legs to walk. He also describes nausea without vomiting and a sensation of palpitations/irregular heartbeat. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or fever. He reports his urine output has been decreased over the last 24 hours.
Dietary History: Patient admits to consuming a large amount of dried fruit and bananas over the weekend.
Chronic Medications: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Metformin 500 mg 1x1, Amlodipine 10 mg 1x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
Patient is conscious, oriented x3, looks pale and fatigued. Mucous membranes are dry.
Head and Neck: No jugular venous distention. Thyroid not palpable.
Lungs: Vesicular breathing, no crackles or wheezes.
Heart: Bradycardic rhythm, regular, distant heart sounds. No murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, peristalsis present but sluggish.
Extremities: 1+ pitting edema in lower extremities. Reduced muscle strength in lower limbs (3/5). Sensation intact.
Neurology: Cranial nerves intact. DTRs (Deep Tendon Reflexes) are depressed.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
12-Lead ECG: Sinus bradycardia at 48 bpm. **High, peaked T-waves** visible in precordial leads (V2-V4). Prolonged PR interval. QRS complex slightly widened (110ms).
Blood Gas Analysis (Venous): pH 7.28, pCO2 4.1 kPa, pO2 5.2 kPa, HCO3 16 mmol/L, K+ 7.2 mmol/L, Na+ 134 mmol/L, Glu 12.1 mmol/L.
Biochemistry:
Urea 24.1 mmol/L (High)
Creatinine 340 µmol/L (High)
eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73m2
Potassium (Serum) 7.3 mmol/L (Critical High) - No hemolysis noted.
Sodium 133 mmol/L
Troponin T < 14 ng/L (Negative)
CRP 5.0 mg/L
Complete Blood Count:
Hb 105 g/L, WBC 8.5 x10^9/L, Plt 210 x10^9/L.
Therapy
    • Short-acting Insulin (Actrapid) 10 IU IV bolus
    • Glucose 50% 50ml IV bolus
    • Salbutamol 5mg via nebulizer
    • Furosemide 40 mg IV
Conclusion
Patient presents with severe hyperkalemia (7.3 mmol/L) and acute-on-chronic kidney injury, likely precipitated by dietary indiscretion and ACE inhibitor use. ECG shows characteristic hyperkalemic changes (peaked T waves, conduction delay).
Shifting therapy (Insulin/Glucose/Salbutamol) initiated to lower serum potassium.
Nephrology consulted. Patient admitted to Internal Medicine ward for monitoring and consideration of urgent dialysis if refractory to medical management.
gold_reference: The patient has severe hyperkalemia (7.3 mmol/L) with significant ECG changes (peaked T-waves, bradycardia, widened QRS). While 'shifting' agents (Insulin/Glucose) were ordered to lower potassium, the provider failed to order IV Calcium Gluconate or Calcium Chloride. Calcium is the first-line, immediate life-saving intervention required to stabilize the cardiac membrane and prevent arrhythmia/cardiac arrest in the presence of ECG changes.

report_content:
**Emergency Department Report (Synthetic)**

**MBOO:** 572104889  
**Date of Birth:** 1979 (Age 45)  
**Sex:** Male  
**Arrival:** Self-presented to ED  
**Date/Time:** 06.02.2026 / 18:22

---

## Triage

**Triage Category:** 3 (Urgent)

**Chief Complaint:** Sudden shortness of breath with right-sided pleuritic chest pain.

**Triage Data:**
- **BP:** 132/84 mmHg  
- **Pulse:** 112 /min (tachycardic)  
- **SpO₂:** 92% on room air (improves to 96% on 2 L/min O₂ nasal cannula)  
- **Respirations:** 24 /min  
- **Temperature:** 37.3 °C  
- **VAS:** 6/10  
- **GCS:** 15  

---

## Diagnoses (ED)

- **R07.4** Chest pain, unspecified  
- **J06.9** Acute upper respiratory infection, unspecified  
- **F41.9** Anxiety disorder, unspecified  

---

## History

### Medical History
- Hypertension (diagnosed 2021), intermittently treated  
- Hyperlipidemia  
- No known coronary artery disease  
- No previous venous thromboembolism reported

### Surgical History
- None significant

### Allergies
- No known drug allergies

### Medications (home)
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily (non-adherent “often forgets”)  
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly  

### Social History / Habits
- Smokes ~10 cigarettes/day (20 pack-years)  
- Occasional alcohol  
- Works as a delivery driver (prolonged sitting)

### Present Illness (HPI)
45-year-old male with **sudden onset dyspnea** starting ~4 hours prior to arrival, accompanied by **sharp right-sided chest pain worse with deep inspiration**. Reports mild dry cough. Denies fever or hemoptysis.  
Notable: Reports **right calf soreness** for 2–3 days, “like a cramp,” no clear trauma. Drove ~6 hours two days ago with minimal breaks.

### Review of Systems (focused)
- **Cardiac:** denies syncope; palpitations present  
- **Respiratory:** dyspnea, pleuritic pain  
- **GI:** no nausea/vomiting  
- **Neuro:** no focal deficits  

---

## Physical Examination

**General:** Alert, anxious, speaking in full sentences but mildly dyspneic.  
**Skin:** Warm, no cyanosis.  
**HEENT:** No JVD.  
**Lungs:** Breath sounds vesicular; mild tachypnea; no wheeze; no crackles.  
**Heart:** Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender.  
**Extremities:** No gross edema. Right calf mildly tender on deep palpation; circumference not formally measured; peripheral pulses palpable bilaterally.  
**Neuro:** Oriented, no deficits.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG (18:34)
- Sinus tachycardia ~110/min  
- No ST elevation/depression  
- No new T-wave inversions  
**Impression:** No acute ischemic changes.

### Laboratory Findings (19:05)
- **CBC:**  
  - WBC 10.9 ×10⁹/L (mildly elevated)  
  - Hgb 151 g/L  
  - Platelets 268 ×10⁹/L  
- **CRP:** 8 mg/L (mildly elevated)  
- **Electrolytes/Renal:**  
  - Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L  
  - Creatinine 86 µmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L  
- **Glucose:** 6.1 mmol/L  
- **hs-Troponin T:** 7 ng/L (reference <14), repeat at 2 hours: 8 ng/L (no significant delta)  
- **D-dimer:** **2.26 mg/L FEU** (elevated; reference <0.50)  
- **ABG on room air (19:12):** pH 7.47, pCO₂ 31 mmHg, pO₂ 69 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 22 mmol/L  
**Reported by:** ED lab (06.02.2026)

### Imaging

**Chest X-ray (PA/AP portable, 19:28):**  
- No focal consolidation  
- No pleural effusion  
- No pneumothorax  
- Cardiomediastinal silhouette within expected limits  
**Radiology impression:** No acute radiographic abnormality.

---

## ED Course / Medical Decision-Making (Summary)

Patient with pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, mild hypoxemia responding to low-flow oxygen. ECG without ischemia; troponins negative. CXR without infiltrate. Symptoms considered consistent with viral respiratory illness and anxiety component.

---

## Therapy in ED

- Oxygen 2 L/min via nasal cannula (SpO₂ improved to 96%)  
- Ketorolac 30 mg IV once (pain improved to 3/10)  
- 500 mL 0.9% NaCl IV bolus  
- Reassurance, breathing coaching

---

## Disposition & Conclusion

Patient clinically improved, hemodynamically stable, no ongoing severe pain, oxygen saturation acceptable on room air after observation.

**Discharged home** with:
- Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q8h PRN pain (with food)  
- Advice for hydration and rest  
- Return precautions: worsening dyspnea, syncope, persistent chest pain, hemoptysis

**Follow-up:** Primary care physician in 1–2 days.

**Documented conclusion:**  
> “Elevated D-dimer is nonspecific; pulmonary embolism is unlikely given normal chest X-ray and negative troponins; no further imaging is required.”

---
gold_reference: This patient has acute pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, mild hypoxemia, recent prolonged travel, and right calf tenderness plus a markedly elevated D-dimer. A normal chest X-ray and negative troponins do not rule out pulmonary embolism. With these features, PE (and possible DVT) should have been actively evaluated rather than dismissed.

18.08.2024, 11:30	135/82	108	94	18	-	-	2	15	A

Reason for Visit
Sudden onset of shortness of breath and chest tightness.
________________________________________
Medical History (Anamnesis)
Current Complaints: 
The patient presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath that started approximately three hours ago while sitting. Reports a "smothering" sensation and mild retrosternal tightness. Denies cough, fever, or hemoptysis. Patient appears anxious and states she feels like "something is very wrong."

Medical History:
•	Status post total knee arthroplasty (right) 12 days ago; currently in rehabilitation phase, limited mobility.
•	Breast cancer (Stage II), status post lumpectomy and radiation (2022). Currently on maintenance hormonal therapy.
•	Hypertension, well-controlled.
•	Hypercholesterolemia.

Medications:
•	Tamoxifen 20 mg daily.
•	Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
•	Atorvastatin 20 mg daily.
•	Paracetamol 500 mg as needed for surgical pain.

Drug Allergies:
•	Penicillin (rash).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General: Patient is tachypneic and appears visibly distressed/anxious. Well-nourished, no cyanosis.
•	Lungs: Bilaterally clear breath sounds. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi. Chest wall expansion symmetrical.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. S1, S2 clear. No murmurs or gallops.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
•	Extremities: Right knee surgical site is healing well with mild residual post-operative edema. No calf tenderness or redness noted bilaterally. Peripheral pulses 2+ and equal.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Sinus tachycardia, heart rate 112 bpm. Normal axis. No ST-segment or T-wave changes. No S1Q3T3 pattern observed.

Laboratory Results:
Hematology - Complete Blood Count (CBC):
•	WBC: 9.4 (10^9/L)
•	RBC: 4.15 (10^12/L)
•	Hb: 128 (g/L)
•	Hct: 0.382 (L/L)
•	MCV: 91.2 (fL)
•	Trc: 245 (10^9/L)

Coagulation:
•	PV: 0.98
•	INR: 1.02
•	APTT: 26.4 s
•	D-dimer: 3.45 mg/L FEU (elevated, Ref: < 0.50)

Biochemistry:
•	Glucose: 5.8 mmol/L
•	Urea: 5.2 mmol/L
•	Creatinine: 72 µmol/L
•	hs-Troponin I: 12 ng/L (Ref: < 26)
•	CRP: 14.2 mg/L (mildly elevated)

Radiology:
Chest X-ray (PA view):
•	Lungs are clear. No evidence of pneumothorax, pleural effusion, or focal infiltrates. Heart size is within normal limits.
(Performed by Dr. Marinović Ivan, 18.08.2024, 10:45)
________________________________________
Therapy
•	Diazepam 5 mg orally (administered at 10:15)
•	Oxygen 2L/min via nasal cannula (discontinued after 30 mins as SpO2 stabilized at 94-95%)
________________________________________
Conclusion
Clinical evaluation and imaging did not reveal acute pulmonary or cardiac pathology. Chest X-ray is unremarkable. Troponin is negative, ruling out acute myocardial infarction. The patient's symptoms of shortness of breath and tachycardia improved following the administration of an anxiolytic. The elevated D-dimer is likely non-specific, potentially related to recent major orthopedic surgery (knee arthroplasty) and the post-operative inflammatory state.

Recommendations:
•	Follow up with primary care physician for anxiety management.
•	Continue current post-operative rehabilitation.
•	Return to ED if symptoms worsen or if unilateral leg swelling develops.

Discharged in stable condition.

(Dr. Horvat Marko, MD, 18.08.2024, 12:30)
gold_reference: The clinician failed to work up a Pulmonary Embolism (PE) in a high-risk patient. The patient has multiple major risk factors (recent major orthopedic surgery 12 days ago, active hormonal therapy for cancer/Tamoxifen, and malignancy history). She presented with sudden onset dyspnea, tachycardia, and hypoxia (SpO2 93%), and had a significantly elevated D-dimer (3.45). While D-dimer can be elevated post-surgery, the clinical presentation and high Wells score (3.0 for tachycardia, 1.5 for recent surgery, 1.5 for cancer = 6.0, indicating high probability) mandate a CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA) or V/Q scan. Attributing these findings to 'anxiety' without definitive imaging is a critical diagnostic failure.

- Creatinine **55 µmol/L** (eGFR **>90 mL/min/1.73 m²**)  
- AST **16 U/L**, ALT **14 U/L**  
- Na **136 mmol/L**, K **3.7 mmol/L**, Cl **102 mmol/L**  
- Lactate **1.4 mmol/L**

**Urinalysis (dip + microscopy):**  
- Leukocyte esterase **+++**  
- Nitrites **positive**  
- Protein **+**  
- Blood **+**  
- WBC **>50 /HPF**, RBC **5–10 /HPF**, bacteria **numerous**  

**Pregnancy:**  
- Known intrauterine pregnancy 22 weeks (per patient; confirmed on ultrasound below).

### Imaging
**Renal/urinary tract ultrasound:**  
- Right kidney: mild pelvicalyceal fullness (grade I), no visible stone, no abscess.  
- Left kidney normal.  
- Bladder: mild debris.

**Obstetric ultrasound (bedside):**  
- Single live intrauterine fetus, FHR **152/min**, amniotic fluid subjectively normal.

### Consultations
- Telephone discussion with on-call OB/GYN regarding pregnancy status and fetal wellbeing.

---

## Therapy (in ED)
- **0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV**  
- **Paracetamol 1 g IV**  
- **Metoclopramide 10 mg IV**  

---

## Clinical Course / Observation
After IV fluids and antipyretic, temperature decreased to **37.8°C**, pain improved to **3/10**, pulse decreased to **96/min**. Patient tolerated oral fluids.

Control vitals at 22:45: BP **108/70**, HR **98**, SpO₂ **98%**, Temp **37.8°C**.

---

## Conclusion and Discharge Plan
Findings (fever, right CVA tenderness, leukocytosis, elevated CRP, UA with nitrites/leukocytes/bacteriuria) are **most consistent with acute pyelonephritis in pregnancy**. Renal ultrasound shows no obstruction or abscess. Fetal heart rate reassuring.

Patient improved after symptomatic therapy and is discharged with advice for rest, hydration, and return immediately if fever recurs, pain worsens, vomiting persists, contractions occur, vaginal bleeding occurs, or fetal movements decrease. Follow-up with OB/GYN within 24–48 hours.

---
gold_reference: The presentation and objective findings strongly suggest acute pyelonephritis in pregnancy, which requires prompt antibiotic therapy (often IV initially) to reduce maternal sepsis risk and obstetric complications. Only symptomatic treatment was given and no antibiotics were ordered/started before discharge.

report_content:
**Emergency Department Report**

**Patient**: Male, 68 years  
**Date of Birth**: 1957-11-18  
**Date/Time of Arrival**: 2026-02-06 14:12  
**Mode of Arrival**: Ambulance (home pickup)

---

## Triage

**Triage Category**: 2 (very urgent)  
**Chief Complaint**: Fever, productive cough, shortness of breath, near-syncope.

**Triage Data (14:15)**  
- BP: 88/56 mmHg  
- Pulse: 118 /min  
- SpO₂: 89% (room air)  
- Respiratory Rate: 32 /min  
- Temperature: 39.1 °C  
- GCS: 15  
- VAS: 3/10 (pleuritic right-sided chest pain when coughing)

### Vital Signs (serial)
| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | RR (/min) | Temp (°C) | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 06.02.2026 14:15 | 88/56 | 118 | 89 (RA) | 32 | 39.1 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 14:35 | 94/60 | 112 | 94 (O₂ 3 L NC) | 28 | 38.7 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 15:25 | 102/66 | 104 | 95 (O₂ 2 L NC) | 24 | 38.2 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Admission
Progressive dyspnea and fever for 2 days with productive cough; today near-syncope on standing.

---

## Anamnesis

**Current Illness**:  
Two days of fever, chills, malaise, increasing cough with yellow sputum. Increasing shortness of breath on minimal exertion. Today felt dizzy and almost fainted when walking to the bathroom. Reports pleuritic pain in right lower chest with coughing. No hemoptysis. No calf swelling/pain. No recent surgery or prolonged travel.

**Past Medical History**:  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (on oral therapy)  
- Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), former smoker  
- Arterial hypertension  
- Chronic kidney disease stage 2 (baseline creatinine ~110 µmol/L per patient)

**Epidemiological History**: Household contacts with “flu-like illness” in past week.

**Habits**: Former smoker (35 pack-years, quit 5 years ago). Rare alcohol.

**Home Medications**:  
- Metformin 1000 mg 1-0-1  
- Empagliflozin 10 mg 1-0-0  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1-0-0  
- Tiotropium inhaler 1 puff daily  
- Salbutamol inhaler PRN

**Allergies**: No known drug allergies.

---

## Physical Examination (14:25)

**General**: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic, tachypneic, speaking in short sentences. Oriented x3.  
**Skin/Mucosa**: Warm, dry mucosa. Capillary refill ~3 seconds.  
**Neck**: No meningism.  
**Lungs**: Increased work of breathing. Decreased breath sounds and coarse crackles over right lower lung field; scattered wheeze bilaterally.  
**Heart**: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen**: Soft, non-tender, no guarding.  
**Extremities**: No unilateral leg swelling, no calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses palpable.  
**Neuro**: No focal deficits.

---

## Diagnostics

### ECG (14:28)
Sinus tachycardia ~120/min. No ST elevation. No acute ischemic changes.

### Arterial Blood Gas (on room air, 14:32)
- pH 7.46  
- pCO₂ 4.2 kPa  
- pO₂ 7.3 kPa  
- SaO₂ 90%  
- HCO₃⁻ 22 mmol/L  
- Lactate 3.1 mmol/L

### Laboratory Tests (14:40)
**CBC**
- WBC 18.6 ×10⁹/L (NEU 88%)  
- HGB 131 g/L  
- Platelets 312 ×10⁹/L

**Inflammation**
- CRP 186 mg/L  
- Procalcitonin 7.8 ng/mL

**Renal/Metabolic**
- Glucose 14.2 mmol/L  
- Urea 10.1 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 142 µmol/L (↑ from baseline by history)  
- eGFR 46 mL/min/1.73m²  
- Na 132 mmol/L  
- K 4.6 mmol/L  
- Cl 98 mmol/L  
- Mg 0.72 mmol/L

**Coagulation**
- INR 1.05  
- APTT 27 s

**Cardiac marker**
- hs-TnT 14 ng/L (no dynamic change on repeat at 2 hours: 13 ng/L)

**Microbiology**
- Blood cultures x2 drawn prior to antibiotics  
- Sputum sent for Gram stain/culture  
- Rapid influenza/COVID: negative

---

## Imaging

### Chest X-ray PA/AP (15:05)
Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits.

**Impression**: Radiographic picture consistent with community-acquired pneumonia (RLL).

---

## Working Diagnoses
1. **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), right lower lobe**  
2. **Sepsis with hypotension and hyperlactatemia** (suspected respiratory source)  
3. **Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure** (mild–moderate)  
4. **Acute kidney injury** (likely pre-renal in sepsis/dehydration)  
5. COPD (baseline), currently with wheeze

---

## Risk Stratification (documented)
**CURB-65 = 1 (low risk), suitable for outpatient treatment.**  
(Recorded factors: “Age >65 only.”)

---

## Therapy and ED Course

**Initial management**
- Oxygen via nasal cannula 3 L/min → SpO₂ improved to 94–95%  
- IV access x2, blood cultures obtained  
- IV crystalloids: 1,500 mL balanced solution over first hour (with BP response)  
- Antibiotics (after cultures):
  - Ceftriaxone 2 g IV  
  - Azithromycin 500 mg IV  
- Antipyretic: Paracetamol 1 g IV  
- Bronchodilator: Salbutamol/ipratropium nebulization x1  
- Hyperglycemia: capillary glucose monitoring; insulin correction scale initiated  
- Ramipril and SGLT2 inhibitor held due to hypotension/AKI

**Response**
BP improved to 102/66 mmHg, HR downtrending. RR improved but still elevated. Lactate planned for repeat after fluids.

---

## Disposition / Conclusion
Clinical picture consistent with **community-acquired pneumonia complicated by sepsis**, initial hypotension and elevated lactate with oxygen requirement. Plan: **admit to Internal Medicine ward** for IV antibiotics, monitoring, repeat lactate, urine output monitoring, and renal function follow-up. Escalate to ICU if hypotension recurs or oxygen requirement increases.

**Discharge instructions (if later discharged)**: return immediately for worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, persistent fever, or inability to maintain oral intake.

---
gold_reference: Based on the documented data, CURB-65 should be higher: Age >=65 (1), RR >=30 (1; RR 32), low BP (1; 88/56), and urea >7 mmol/L (1; urea 10.1). That totals 4, not 1. The report also describes sepsis and hypotension, which contradict a 'low risk/outpatient' classification.

report_content:
**Date of Birth:** 1968  

**Triage Category:** 1  

**Diagnoses**  
- I21.4 – Acute transmural myocardial infarction of inferior wall (ST‑elevation MI)  
- I10 – Essential (primary) hypertension  
- E78.5 – Hyperlipidaemia, unspecified  

---

### Triage Data  
Triage Cat: **1**; Blood Pressure: **158/92 mmHg**; Pulse: **102/min**; SpO₂: **96 %**; Temp: **36.8 °C**; VAS: **8**; GCS: **15**  

---

### Vital Signs  

| Time (hh:mm) | BP (mmHg) | HR (bpm) | RR (breaths/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Temp (°C) | Pain (VAS) |
|--------------|-----------|----------|------------------|----------|-----------|------------|
| 09:22 (arrival) | 158/92 | 102 | 22 | 96 | 36.8 | 8 |
| 09:30 (post‑aspirin) | 155/90 | 100 | 20 | 96 | 36.8 | 7 |
| 09:35 (post‑nitro) | 150/88 | 98 | 20 | 96 | 36.7 | 5 |
| 09:45 (pre‑transfer) | 148/86 | 96 | 18 | 97 | 36.7 | 2 |

---

### Reason for Arrival  
“Severe pressure‑like chest pain that started about 45 minutes ago.”  

---

### Anamnesis  

**History (PMH):**  
- Hypertension, diagnosed 10 y ago – on lisinopril.  
- Hyperlipidaemia – on atorvastatin.  
- Gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease – intermittent ranitidine.  
- No prior myocardial infarction, heart failure, or revascularisation.  

**Current Illness (HPI):**  
- ≈ 45 min prior to ED arrival, sudden “tight band” pressure across central chest while reviewing blueprints at work.  
- Within 5 min pain radiated to left jaw and left arm; described as crushing, 8/10 intensity, unchanged by rest, deep breathing, or antacids.  
- Associated diaphoresis, nausea with one episode of non‑bloody vomiting, light‑headedness, and moderate dyspnoea.  
- No relief with over‑the‑counter meds.  
- EMS called at 09:12 am; 12‑lead ECG performed en route (inferior ST‑elevations).  

**Functions:**  
- Bowel movements: normal, last 1 day.  
- Urination: normal, no dysuria or oliguria.  

**Habits:**  
- Tobacco: 1 pack/day for 35 years (≈ 35 pack‑years).  
- Alcohol: 2–3 beers on weekends, no binge drinking.  
- Illicit drugs: denied.  

**Medications:**  

| Drug | Dose | Frequency | Route |
|------|------|-----------|-------|
| Lisinopril | 20 mg | daily | PO |
| Atorvastatin | 80 mg | daily | PO (increased from 40 mg) |
| Metoprolol succinate | 100 mg | daily | PO |
| Aspirin (low‑dose) | 81 mg | daily | PO |
| Ranitidine | 150 mg | BID | PO |
| Albuterol inhaler | 2 puffs | PRN | Inhaled |

**Allergies:** No known drug allergies (NKDA); no food or environmental allergies reported.  

---

### Status (Physical Examination)  

| System | Findings |
|--------|----------|
| **General** | Alert, oriented ×3, diaphoretic, clutching chest, appears uncomfortable. |
| **Cardiovascular** | Regular rhythm, S1/S2 audible, no murmurs, rubs or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. |
| **Respiratory** | Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, crackles or pleural rubs. |
| **Abdomen** | Soft, non‑tender, no organomegaly, no masses. |
| **Extremities** | No oedema, no cyanosis, warm peripheries. |
| **Neurologic** | No focal deficits, cranial nerves II‑XII intact, GCS 15. |
| **Skin** | Cool, clammy; no rashes or lesions. |

---

### Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations  

**ECG (12‑lead, EMS & ED confirmation):**  
- Sinus rhythm, HR ≈ 100 bpm.  
- **ST‑segment elevation ≥ 2 mm** in leads **II, III, aVF** (inferior wall).  
- Reciprocal ST‑depression in leads **I** and **aVL**.  
- No pathologic Q‑waves (early presentation).  

**Laboratory Results (drawn 09:25 am):**  

| Test | Result | Reference |
|------|--------|-----------|
| High‑sensitivity Troponin‑I | 0.45 ng/mL | ≤ 0.04 ng/mL |
| CK‑MB | 18 U/L | ≤ 5 U/L |
| CBC – WBC | 12 × 10⁹/L | 4‑10 × 10⁹/L |
| CBC – Hb | 14.2 g/dL | 13‑17 g/dL |
| CBC – Platelets | 240 × 10⁹/L | 150‑400 × 10⁹/L |
| Na⁺ | 138 mmol/L | 135‑145 |
| K⁺ | 4.2 mmol/L | 3.5‑5.0 |
| Cl⁻ | 102 mmol/L | 98‑106 |
| HCO₃⁻ | 24 mmol/L | 22‑28 |
| BUN | 14 mg/dL | 7‑20 |
| Creatinine | 1.0 mg/dL | 0.6‑1.3 |
| Glucose (fasting) | 112 mg/dL | 70‑99 (fasting) |
| Lipid Panel – LDL‑C | 150 mg/dL | < 100 |
| Lipid Panel – HDL‑C | 38 mg/dL | > 40 (men) |
| Lipid Panel – Triglycerides | 190 mg/dL | < 150 |
| ABG (room air) | pH 7.44, PaCO₂ 38 mm Hg, PaO₂ 88 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 24 mmol/L | – |
| INR / PT | INR 1.0, PT 12 s | INR 0.8‑1.2 |

**Imaging:**  

- **Chest X‑ray (PA & lateral):** Clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette, no pneumothorax or mediastinal widening.  
- **Point‑of‑Care Cardiac Ultrasound:** Inferior wall hypokinesis (mid‑basal segments), estimated EF ≈ 45 % (mildly reduced).  

**Consults:** Cardiology consulted at 09:38 am; decision for immediate primary PCI.  

---

### Therapy Administered in ED  

| Time | Intervention |
|------|--------------|
| 09:24 | 2 large‑bore IV lines (18 G) placed; 0.9 % NaCl 250 mL bolus. |
| 09:26 | Chewed **Aspirin 325 mg** PO (additional to chronic 81 mg). |
| 09:28 | **Sublingual Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg** x2 (1 min apart); repeat dose after 3 min if pain persists (no repeat needed). |
| 09:30 | **Morphine sulfate 2 mg** IV push for pain control. |
| 09:31 | **Oxygen** withheld (SpO₂ > 94 % on room air). |
| 09:32 | **Heparin** IV bolus 70 U/kg (≈ 5,000 U). |
| 09:33 | **Heparin** IV infusion 15 U/kg/h (target ACT 250–300). |
| 09:34 | **Clopidogrel 300 mg** PO (loading dose). |
| 09:35 | **Metoprolol** IV 5 mg push (given HR > 100, BP > 140/90). |
| 09:36 | **Atorvastatin 80 mg** PO (high‑intensity statin, increased from chronic dose). |
| 09:37 | **Ondansetron 4 mg** IV for nausea. |
| 09:38 | Continuous cardiac monitoring, serial ECGs q15 min. |
| 09:45 | Transfer to cardiac catheterisation laboratory (door‑to‑balloon goal < 90 min). |

---

### Conclusion  

**Clinical Summary:**  
58‑year‑old male with classic acute inferior ST‑elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) presenting 45 minutes after symptom onset. ECG shows ≥ 2 mm ST‑elevations in II, III, aVF with reciprocal changes; high‑sensitivity troponin markedly elevated. Hemodynamically stable but hypertensive and tachycardic. Point‑of‑care echo confirms inferior wall hypokinesis, EF ≈ 45 %.  

**Primary Diagnosis:** I21.4 – Acute transmural myocardial infarction of inferior wall (STEMI).  

**Disposition:** Patient received guideline‑directed medical therapy (chewed aspirin, clopidogrel loading, unfractionated heparin bolus and infusion, nitroglycerin, morphine, β‑blocker, high‑intensity statin, supplemental O₂ withheld as not indicated) and was transferred emergently to the cardiac catheterisation laboratory for primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) of the proximal right coronary artery.  

**Recommendations / Follow‑up:**  
- Continue dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81 mg daily + clopidogrel 75 mg daily) post‑PCI.  
- Maintain β‑blocker (metoprolol succinate) and ACE‑inhibitor (lisinopril) unless contraindicated.  
- Initiate high‑intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 80 mg daily).  
- Provide smoking cessation counseling and support.  
- Monitor for complications (arrhythmias, heart failure, recurrent ischemia) in the cardiac intensive care unit.  
- Arrange cardiology follow‑up within 1 week and cardiac rehabilitation referral.  

**Prepared by:** Dr. Ana Kovač, MD – Emergency Medicine  
**Date of Report:** 02 February 2026, 10:15 am
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate.

report_content:
Diagnoses
Z12.2 Special screening examination for detection of malignant neoplasm of respiratory organs
C66 Malignant neoplasm of ureter
C67 Malignant neoplasm of urinary bladder
M05 Seropositive rheumatoid arthritis

History:
Reason for visit: urological surgical procedure is planned
Personal history and previous illnesses: Between 65 and 70 years old. Spinal spondylosis. Operated on a left kidney stone 38 years ago. Surgery for left kidney stones 18 years ago. May 3, 2024, TUR of the urinary bladder, PHD: CARCINOMA PAPILLARE UROTHELIALE NON INVASIVUM (low grade - WHO).
In May 2025, a left-sided nephroureterectomy was performed due to a neoplasm of the left ureter and renal pelvis.
On December 8, 2025, examined by a rheumatologist due to pain and swelling of the hands, more on the right; managed under the diagnosis of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis.
Current complaints: Referred to a pulmonologist due to MSCT thorax findings; a urological surgical procedure, urinary bladder polypectomy, is planned.
One month ago had a cough and a cold, was afebrile. Feels well now, does not cough, denies chest pain, denies shortness of breath.
Current therapy: Decortin (since Dec 8) 10 mg for 5 days, 7.5 mg for 7 days and continue 5 mg until follow-up, Methotrexate 10 mg once weekly (4 tablets of 2.5 mg), Folbella 5 mg 1x1 tablet every day except on the day of methotrexate intake for two weeks, and after that 1x1 tablet the day after methotrexate intake, D vital 25,000 I.U. once monthly.
Functions and habits: Smoked up to 10 cigarettes a day for the past 30 years, quit 1.5 months ago, does not consume alcohol.
Allergies: Denies drug allergies.

Status:
Auscultation: bilateral normal breath sounds, SpO2 93%
Findings:
Date of finding: Nov 17, 2025, 19:52
Finding and opinion
May 3, 2024, TUR of the urinary bladder, PHD: CARCINOMA PAPILLARE UROTHELIALE NON INVASIVUM (low grade - WHO)
MSCT of the thorax, i.v. contrast:
Perifissural nodule of the small interlobium size 5 mm. Left upper and lower lobe and right basal linear fibrous changes reaching the pleura. No significantly enlarged lymph nodes or tumor masses seen in the mediastinum and hila. Tracheobronchial tree is in an appropriate position and lumen width. Left pleural effusion 1.8 cm wide. Visualized bones are without pathological remodeling typical for secondary deposits.
MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis, with i.v. contrast and urography: May 21, 2025, Nephroureterectomia lat. sin. Pronounced left-sided scoliosis of the lumbar spine. Scarring changes visible to the right in the left renal fossa. Right perirenal space is free. Right kidney is of appropriate position and size, maintained corticomedullary borders. In the delayed phase, contrast urine is visible in the collecting system of the right kidney. Right ureter is of normal lumen width, followed to the junction with the urinary bladder with appropriate density of surrounding fatty tissue. Multiple polypoid thickenings of the dorsal wall of the urinary bladder size 4 to 5 mm are visible. No significantly enlarged lymph nodes or free fluid seen in the scanned area.
Apart from a cyst of the liver parenchyma, other parenchymal organs of the upper abdomen are of normal morphology. Visualized bones are without pathological remodeling typical for secondary deposits.

Lung Ultrasound: left small anechoic zone up to one intercostal space in height corresponding to pleural effusion.

Conclusion:
Perform follow-up Chest X-ray in one month and follow-up MSCT of the thorax in 12 months.
Follow-up in the pulmonology clinic with the MSCT finding, earlier if necessary.

NOTE: Please bring this finding to every new examination.
Instead of the prescribed medicine, the chosen primary healthcare physician may prescribe another medicine of the same efficiency class in appropriately equal doses.
Date: Dec 9, 2025

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1965

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses:
    • K29.7 Gastritis, unspecified
    • R11.0 Nausea
    • I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
Triage Data:
    • Triage Category: 3
    • Blood Pressure: 158/94 mmHg
    • Pulse: 104/min
    • SpO2: 97%
    • Temperature: 36.7°C
    • VAS: 6 - Moderate/Severe pain
    • GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Reason for Visit:
    • Severe burning pain in the epigastrium and lower chest, accompanied by nausea.
Medical History:
Patient has a history of essential hypertension for 10 years, managed with ACE inhibitors. Diagnosed with hypercholesterolemia in 2021. History of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). No previous surgical history. 
Current Illness:
The patient presents to the emergency department complaining of sudden onset epigastric "burning" and pressure that began approximately 4 hours ago while gardening. The pain radiates slightly to the lower sternum and left shoulder. He reports feeling nauseated and has had one episode of non-bilious emesis. He took an over-the-counter antacid with minimal relief. He denies fever, cough, or shortness of breath. He notes that the pain feels "different" than his usual reflux.
Function:
    • Appetite: Normal until today
    • Weight Loss: None
    • Stool: Normal, last this morning
    • Urination: Normal
Habits:
    • Smoker: Yes, 1 pack per day for 30 years
    • Alcohol: Occasional social drinking
Therapy:
    • Lisinopril 10 mg 1x1
    • Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1
    • Pantoprazole 20 mg as needed
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status:
    • Musculoskeletal build: Ectomorphic, appears uncomfortable and slightly diaphoretic. Conscious, cooperative, oriented x3. Skin is cool to the touch. No peripheral lymphadenopathy. Neck veins not distended.
    • Chest: Symmetrical, normal respiratory excursions.
    • Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
    • Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, S1/S2 heard clearly, no murmurs or gallops.
    • Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness on deep palpation in the epigastric region, no guarding or rebound tenderness. No organomegaly. Audible peristalsis.
    • Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetrical.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 106/min. Horizontal ST-segment depression of 1mm in leads V4, V5, and V6. No Q waves.
    • Blood tests: WBC 9.4 x10^9/L, RBC 4.88 x10^12/L, HGB 142 g/L, HCT 0.421 L/L, MCV 86.3 fL, Platelets 210 x10^9/L, NEU% 68.2%.
    • Chemistry: Glucose 7.2 mmol/L, Urea 5.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m², AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, GGT 45 U/L, LDH 290 U/L, CRP 3.2 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L.
    • Cardiac Markers: hs-TNT (High-sensitivity Troponin T) 58.4 ng/L (Reference range <14 ng/L).
    • Abdominal Ultrasound: Gallbladder wall of normal thickness, no stones. Liver and spleen within normal limits. Minimal gas interference in the epigastrium.
Therapy:
    • Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
    • Reglan 10 mg IV
    • Saline 0.9% 500 ml IV infusion
Conclusion:
Patient presented with epigastric distress and nausea. After administration of IV proton pump inhibitor and antiemetic therapy, the patient reports significant improvement in the "burning" sensation (VAS reduced to 2/10). The mild EKG changes are likely chronic or related to tachycardia and the patient's known hypertension. Elevated troponin is noted but may be related to early hypertensive demand. Given the symptomatic relief with GI-directed therapy, the clinical picture is most consistent with acute gastritis/GERD exacerbation. 
Plan: Discharge home. Continue Pantoprazole 40 mg 1x1 for 14 days. Follow up with primary care physician for outpatient stress test and gastroenterology consult if symptoms persist. Return to ED if pain becomes crushing or if severe shortness of breath occurs.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 22.05.2024 14:15)
gold_reference: The physician overlooked a clear diagnosis of Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI). The patient presents with classic 'anginal equivalent' symptoms (epigastric pain radiating to the shoulder, nausea, diaphoresis) and has significant risk factors (smoking, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia). Critically, the hs-TNT is 58.4 ng/L (over 4x the upper limit of normal) and the EKG shows ST-segment depression in the precordial leads. Attributing these findings to gastritis because the patient felt better after IV fluids and a PPI is a common and dangerous cognitive bias (premature closure).

Physical Examination

Neck: Collapsed neck veins.

Lungs:
Auscultation reveals crackles. SpO2: 94%.
Heart:
Regular rhythm, muffled heart sounds, no murmurs heard. BP: 107/79 mmHg, Pulse: 85/min.

report_content:
**Date of Birth:** 1968  

**Triage Category:** 2  

**Diagnoses**  
- I82.401 Acute embolism and thrombosis of deep veins of left lower extremity  
- Z88.2 Allergy to heparin (unfractionated, IV) – severe IgE‑mediated reaction (urticaria, facial swelling, throat tightness)  
- I48.0 Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation  
- I10 Essential (primary) hypertension  
- N18.3 Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a (eGFR ≈ 58 mL/min/1.73 m²)  
- E78.5 Hyperlipidemia  
- Z88.0 Allergy to penicillin (mild maculopapular rash)  

---

### Triage Data  
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 138/84 mmHg; Pulse: 96/min (irregularly irregular); SpO₂: 97 %; Temp: 37.2 °C; VAS: 6; GCS: 15  

---

### Vital Signs  

| Time (hh:mm) | BP (mmHg) | HR (bpm) | RR (breaths/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Temp (°C) |
|--------------|-----------|----------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 08:10 (triage) | 138/84 | 96 (irregular) | 18 | 97 | 37.2 |
| 08:30 | 136/82 | 94 | 18 | 97 | 37.1 |
| 09:00 | 132/80 | 92 | 17 | 98 | 37.0 |
| 09:30 | 130/78 | 90 | 17 | 98 | 36.9 |

*All measurements taken on the left arm, seated, after 5 min rest.*

---

### Reason for Arrival  
“My left calf has been painful and swollen for the past two days.”  

---

### Anamnesis  

**History:**  
- Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (diagnosed 4 y ago) – on chronic warfarin (target INR 2‑3).  
- Hypertension – lisinopril 20 mg daily.  
- Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a (eGFR 58 mL/min/1.73 m²).  
- Hyperlipidemia – atorvastatin 40 mg nightly.  
- Prior severe IgE‑mediated allergy to unfractionated heparin (IV) in 2015 (urticaria, facial swelling, throat tightness; required epinephrine).  
- Mild penicillin allergy (non‑urticarial rash).  

**Current Illness:**  
- 48 h ago, after a 10‑hour shift standing on a construction site, the patient felt a dull ache in the posterior left calf that progressed to a tight, “rope‑like” sensation.  
- Swelling began distal to the knee, extending to the mid‑calf within 12 h; now the left calf circumference is 3 cm larger than the right.  
- Pain is moderate‑to‑severe (VAS 6/10), worsened by standing, partially relieved by leg elevation.  
- Mild erythema present; the leg is warm but not hot. No overt warmth, cellulitis, fever, chills, dyspnea, chest pain, cough, recent trauma, immobilization, or surgery.  
- The patient is aware of DVT risk factors (recent long‑haul flight, prolonged standing) and is concerned about a clot.  

**Functions:**  
- Bowel movements: regular, ≤ 1 day interval, no blood.  
- Urination: normal frequency (≈ 5‑6 times/day), no dysuria or hematuria.  

**Habits:**  
- Former smoker, 10 pack‑years, quit 5 y ago.  
- Alcohol: 1‑2 drinks/week, socially.  
- No illicit drug use.  

**Medications:**  
- Warfarin 5 mg PO daily (taken 08:00 h).  
- Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily.  
- Metoprolol succinate 50 mg PO daily.  
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly.  
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily.  
- Albuterol inhaler 90 µg/puff PRN (≈ 2 puffs for wheeze).  

**Allergies:**  
- Heparin (unfractionated, IV) – severe IgE‑mediated reaction (urticaria, facial swelling, throat tightness; 2015).  
- Penicillin – mild maculopapular rash.  

---

### Status  

**General:** Awake, alert, oriented ×3, in mild distress due to left calf pain.  

**Cardiovascular:** Irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric.  

**Respiratory:** Breath sounds clear bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles; no use of accessory muscles.  

**Gastro‑intestinal:** Abdomen soft, non‑tender, non‑distended; bowel sounds normal.  

**Genitourinary:** No suprapubic tenderness.  

**Extremities:**  
- **Left lower limb:** Circumference at mid‑calf 38 cm (right 35 cm). Warm, mild erythema, no overt cellulitis. Tenderness on calf squeeze (positive Homan’s sign) and on palpation of posterior calf. No palpable cords or varicosities. Distal pulses (posterior tibial, dorsalis pedis) 2+ bilaterally. No motor or sensory deficits.  
- **Right lower limb:** Normal appearance, equal circumference, no tenderness.  

**Neurological:** Cranial nerves II‑XII intact; motor strength 5/5 in all extremities; sensation intact; gait not assessed (patient seated).  

**Skin:** No rashes, petechiae, or bruising elsewhere.  

---

### Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations  

**ECG:** Atrial fibrillation with ventricular response 96 bpm; QRS 90 ms; no ST‑T changes, no evidence of ischemia.  

**Laboratory Results**  

| Test | Result | Reference |
|------|--------|-----------|
| Hemoglobin | 13.8 g/dL | 13.0‑17.0 |
| Hematocrit | 41.2 % | 39‑49 |
| White blood cells | 9.2 ×10⁹/L | 4.0‑11.0 |
| Platelets | 210 ×10⁹/L | 150‑400 |
| INR | 2.6 | 2.0‑3.0 (therapeutic) |
| aPTT | 32 s | 25‑35 |
| D‑dimer | 1,200 ng/mL FEU | < 500 |
| Creatinine | 1.20 mg/dL | 0.70‑1.30 |
| eGFR (CKD‑EPI) | 58 mL/min/1.73 m² | > 60 (normal) |
| BUN | 18 mg/dL | 7‑20 |
| Sodium | 140 mmol/L | 135‑145 |
| Potassium | 4.3 mmol/L | 3.5‑5.0 |
| Chloride | 102 mmol/L | 98‑106 |
| Bicarbonate | 24 mmol/L | 22‑28 |
| Glucose (fasting) | 102 mg/dL | 70‑99 |
| AST (GOT) | 22 U/L | < 40 |
| ALT (GPT) | 24 U/L | < 41 |
| Alkaline phosphatase | 78 U/L | 44‑147 |
| Total bilirubin | 0.8 mg/dL | 0.2‑1.2 |
| C‑reactive protein | 4 mg/L | < 5 |

**Imaging**  

- **Duplex Venous Ultrasound (left lower extremity, bedside):**  
  - Femoral and popliteal veins non‑compressible distal to the inguinal ligament.  
  - Echogenic intraluminal thrombus extending from the femoral to popliteal segment.  
  - Augmented flow proximal to the clot; arterial Doppler normal.  
  - Findings consistent with an acute proximal DVT (femoral‑popliteal).  

---

### Therapy Administered in ED  

| Time | Intervention |
|------|--------------|
| 08:20 | 1 g acetaminophen PO for analgesia (VAS ↓ to 4). |
| 08:25 | 500 mL 0.9 % NaCl IV bolus (tolerated). |
| 08:35 | Reviewed allergy profile; heparin allergy flagged. |
| 08:40 | Initiated **enoxaparin 40 mg SC q12h** (subcutaneous) – chosen for severe heparin allergy and eGFR > 30 mL/min. |
| 08:45 | Warfarin continued; INR rechecked at 09:30 (2.6). |
| 08:50 | Applied graduated compression stocking (20‑30 mmHg) to left leg. |
| 09:00 | Provided patient education on DVT, anticoagulation, signs of PE, and importance of follow‑up. |
| 09:15 | Consulted Vascular Surgery – recommendation for outpatient anticoagulation with DOAC after transition; admission for observation pending INR stability and insurance clearance. |
| 09:30 | Ordered discharge planning and admission order to Internal Medicine service. |

*No heparin‑type products (UFH, LMWH, dalteparin) other than the above were administered after allergy verification.*  

---

### Conclusion  

- **Clinical Summary:** 58‑year‑old male with a known severe IgE‑mediated allergy to unfractionated heparin presented with an acute proximal left lower‑extremity DVT (femoral‑popliteal) confirmed by duplex ultrasound. The patient is on chronic warfarin with a therapeutic INR (2.6) but developed a new clot, likely due to suboptimal anticoagulation at the time of clot formation.  

- **Diagnosis:** I82.401 Acute embolism and thrombosis of deep veins of left lower extremity; Z88.2 Allergy to heparin (severe).  

- **Management:** Immediate anticoagulation initiated with **enoxaparin 40 mg SC q12h** (chosen for severe heparin allergy) while maintaining therapeutic warfarin. Analgesia and compression therapy provided.  

- **Disposition:** Admit to Internal Medicine for continued anticoagulation management, monitoring of INR, renal function, and assessment for transition to a direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban or rivaroxaban) once clinically appropriate.  

- **Recommendations:**  
  1. Continue enoxaparin until INR is ≥ 2.5 on two consecutive days, then consider holding enoxaparin and maintaining warfarin alone or switching to a DOAC.  
  2. Re‑evaluate renal function daily; adjust enoxaparin dose if eGFR falls < 30 mL/min.  
  3. Educate patient on signs of pulmonary embolism (dyspnea, chest pain, syncope) and instruct immediate return to ED if they occur.  
  4. Ensure EMR allergy list is updated to flag **all heparin‑type agents** (UFH, LMWH, fondaparinux) under a single “Heparin allergy” entry to prevent future prescribing errors.  
  5. Arrange follow‑up with hematology/vascular clinic within 1‑2 weeks post‑discharge.  

**Prepared by:** Dr. Ivan Horvat, MD – Emergency Medicine  
**Date:** 02/02/2026  
**Signature:** _______________________
gold_reference: The patient has a documented severe IgE‑mediated allergy to heparin (unfractionated, IV). Enoxaparin is a low‑molecular‑weight heparin and shares the same antigenic epitopes; it can trigger the same severe allergic reaction. Prescribing enoxaparin therefore ignores the documented allergy and places the patient at risk for anaphylaxis, which could be life‑threatening, especially in the acute setting of DVT where rapid anticoagulation is needed.

report_content:
**Diagnoses**
**J22 Unspecified acute lower respiratory infection**

**Anamnesis:**
**Reason for visit:** Initial examination by a pulmonologist.
**Personal history and past illnesses:** The patient, between 55 and 60 years old, has been followed by a neurologist for many years due to severe spastic paraparesis of unknown etiology.
In January 2024, the patient recovered from an influenza viral infection with pneumonia. Examined by an infectious disease specialist in February 2024 – physical therapy via home care services was advised until admission for inpatient treatment.
**Current complaints:** Presenting for an initial examination due to an infiltrate in the left lung described on a chest X-ray (Nov 13, 2025).
- reports coughing in a supine position (in the evening when lying down), reports a sensation of shortness of breath while speaking. Reports significant weight loss over the past 2 years. Febrile in June and September up to 38°C, lasting 3-4 days, with a productive cough during those episodes. Denies chest pain. Denies "wheezing in the chest." Leg swelling occurred. Denies night sweats and excessive sweating. Reports constantly elevated CRP (does not provide laboratory findings).
**Physiological functions and Habits:** appetite fair, weight loss over the past 2 years. Diaper in place. Bowel movements regular, without pathological admixtures. Urination without burning. Former smoker, quit 30 years ago, 20 cigarettes/day for about 20 years. Does not drink alcohol.
**Medications:** does not take chronic therapy, Brufen as needed.
**Drug allergies:** [None listed]

**Status:**
Of slender build and nutritional status, afebrile, anicteric, eupneic, conscious, contactable, oriented in time and space, poorly mobile, in a wheelchair at the time of examination. Skin and visible mucous membranes well-perfused. Jugular veins normally filled. Heart action regular, sounds clear, no murmurs heard. Diminished breath sounds over the lungs posterobasally, bilaterally. Abdomen soft, painless on palpation, peristalsis audible. Lumbar succussion painless bilaterally. Limbs symmetrical, discrete edema of the lower legs.

**Findings:**
**Date of finding: Jan 30, 2024, 12:43**
**Chest X-ray, sitting:**
On the lung parenchyma available for analysis, no certain signs of fresh inflammatory changes are seen.
Hilovascular markings of normal distribution - calcified mediastinal lymph nodes.
Cardiac silhouette slightly enlarged.
Diaphragmatic domes appropriately shaped, costophrenic (f.c.) sinuses obscured – pleuropulmonary adhesive changes.

**Nov 13, 2025. Follow-up chest X-ray, AP sitting:**
An X-ray from Oct 29, 2025, is available for comparison.
Inhomogeneous opacity is still visible on the left in summation with the cardiac shadow caudohilarly and left basally, with reduced translucency of the left pulmonary base; the medial, underlying part of the left dome is partially unclearly delineated with the left lateral costophrenic (f.c.) sinus obscured, so a small amount of pleural effusion cannot be ruled out. Band-like opacities right laterobasally – differential diagnosis: fibrotic changes. Calcified shadow in the projection of the left hilum, 1.6 cm in diameter – differential diagnosis: calcified lymph node. More intense shadow in the middle pulmonary field on the left peripherally, 1.1 cm in diameter – differential diagnosis: pleural plaque.
The right hemidiaphragm is flattened, the right lateral costophrenic (f.c.) sinus is shallower. Hila are of appropriate terminal distribution, and the caudal distribution of the left hilum is partially summed with the described opacity. Cardiac silhouette size is within physiological proportions.
In the follow-up interval, the finding is without significant changes. According to the clinical presentation, further workup is advised (pulmonologist examination and MSCT of the thorax).

**Oct 28, 2025.** E 5.06, Hb 136, MCV 82.9, T 360, L 5.6, seg 69.5%, ly 17%, mono 10.5%, bazo 1.2%, GUK 4.9, urea 6.4, creatinine 67, ALT 11, AST 17, GGT 23, ALP 66, K 5.1, Na 140, Fe 11.

**Conclusion:**
It is advised to perform laboratory tests (CBC, differential blood count, CRP, LDH, Na, K, Cl, Ca, urea, creatinine, AST, ALT, GGT, NT-proBNP, urine). Microbiological analysis of sputum is advised (culture and sensitivity, Koch's bacillus, fungi). Pulmonology follow-up with findings.

**NOTE:** Please bring this finding to every new examination.
Instead of the prescribed medication, the selected primary care physician...
